entry.title: Inspection lot sample size calculation based on manually entered carton quantity
entry.content:
Hi experts,

We have a requirement in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition related to QM sampling.

At goods receipt, users manually enter the actual carton quantity. For example:

  • GR quantity: 1000 PCS
  • Actual cartons: 9 CTN (stored as batch characteristic or custom field)

In the inspection plan, we have an inspection characteristic for outer carton inspection. The expectation is that the sample size should be calculated based on the carton quantity (e.g. 9 CTN), not the inspection lot quantity (1000 PCS).

However, standard sampling seems to always use the inspection lot quantity, and alternative units require a fixed conversion, which we do not have (carton quantity varies per GR).

We would like to confirm:

  1. Is it possible in standard S/4HANA Cloud to calculate sample size based on a manually entered carton quantity?
  2. Can sampling be driven by batch characteristics or custom fields?
  3. If not, is this a standard limitation?
  4. Are there any recommended approaches (e.g. physical samples, sample-drawing procedures, etc.)?

Expected result: sample size for carton inspection is based on 9 CTN instead of 1000 PCS.

Thanks in advance for your help!

entry.title: One Configuration Switch Unlocks Invoice Receipt Date Tracking for Every Country
entry.content:
For years, the Invoice Receipt Date on the accounting document header was a assigned to a handful of country localizations like the Czech VAT Ledger. With SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2608, that changes: one configuration switch now activates the field for every company code in every country/region.

Scope

Scope Note: This blog post covers SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2608 and later. The feature is delivered through Configuration Activity 107263 — Activate Invoice Receipt Date, application component FI-LOC-FI, and applies to scope item J60 (Accounts Payable). The activation is a customer-managed setting: no SAP Note or manual coding is required.

Why the Invoice Receipt Date Matters

The Invoice Receipt Date — stored in the classic field BKPF-REINDAT — captures the day your organization actually received a supplier invoice. It is deliberately distinct from the three dates you already know:

  • Document Date (BLDAT) — the date printed on the supplier's invoice.
  • Posting Date (BUDAT) — the date that drives the accounting period.
  • Entry Date (CPUDT) — the system timestamp when the document was entered.

Legislation and internal governance increasingly measure payment performance from the moment the invoice arrives at your door — not from the invoice date and not from the posting date. Examples include the EU Late Payment Directive, the UK Payment Practices Reporting obligation for large companies, and dozens of country-specific VAT and audit rules. Without a dedicated field, teams end up storing the receipt date in a custom Z-field, misusing the baseline date, or reconstructing it from workflow logs — none of which reports cleanly.

Activating the standard field solves all three problems at once: it becomes visible in every relevant Fiori app, it flows into journal entries automatically, and it is available as a first-class field for CDS views, custom reports, and statutory extractors.

Activation in Three Screens

The activation is a linear flow: open the configuration activity, pick the company codes that need it, and post an invoice to confirm the field is live.

Step 1 — Open Configuration Activity 107263

In your configuration environment, search for Activate Invoice Receipt Date (Configuration Activity 107263). The SSCUI is the single entry point for this setting.

Step 2 — Select the Company Codes

Add each company code that should expose the Invoice Receipt Date field. The activation is per company code, so you can pilot the feature in one entity before rolling it out group-wide.

Step 3 — Post a Supplier Invoice to Verify

Open Create Supplier Invoice (F0859) — or any of the FI/AP posting apps — and the new Invoice Receipt Date field appears alongside the Document Date and Posting Date. Once filled, the value is persisted in BKPF-REINDAT and is immediately available for reporting.

Practical Takeaway

To roll this out cleanly:

  • Identify the company codes that need the Invoice Receipt Date (start with entities subject to statutory payment-practice reporting).
  • Open Configuration Activity 107263 in the configuration environment.
  • Activate the setting for the selected company codes.
  • Test with one supplier invoice in F0859 — confirm the field appears and posts to BKPF-REINDAT.
  • Align your AP workflow so the mailroom / OCR / IDP tool populates the field automatically for every incoming invoice.

Summary

The Invoice Receipt Date has finally graduated from a niche public-sector field to a standard journal-entry attribute available to every SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition customer. One SSCUI, one flag per company code, and your organization has a compliance-ready foundation for payment-practice reporting and receipt-based payment KPIs — without a custom field in sight.

Key Takeaways

  • Global availability: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2608 makes the Invoice Receipt Date (BKPF-REINDAT) available in all countries/regions, not just Public Sector or a few localizations.
  • Customer-controlled activation: Configuration Activity 107263 — Activate Invoice Receipt Date turns the field on per company code — no SAP Note, no code change.
  • Standard field, standard reporting: Because the value lives in BKPF-REINDAT, it flows into CDS views, extractors, and statutory reports as a first-class attribute — retiring the custom Z-fields many customers have relied on for years.

More Information

  • SAP Help Portal — Activate Invoice Receipt Date for All Countries/Regions (What's New 2608)
  • SAP Help Portal — Activating the Invoice Receipt Date (Country/Region-Specific Functions)
  • Scope item J60 — Accounts Payable
  • Application component FI-LOC-FI

Published by SAP Globalization Services · Feedback and questions welcome in the comments below.

entry.title: Klonen - aus eins mach viele: Einheit 2 Lektion 3
entry.content:
An English version of this lesson is available at: Get Coding with Snap! - Building Up to AI

 

Videolektion "Klonen"

In dieser Lektion beschäftigst du dich mit Klonen, um aus einem Objekt ganze Schwärme von Agenten zu erzeugen. Du erfährst außerdem mehr über den Geltungsbereich von Variablen, den sogenannten Scope, und wie du damit effizient Variablen über verschiedene Klone hinweg verwenden kannst. 

Folge den Schritten aus dem Video in Snap!. Die Aufgaben bauen teilweise auf das Projekt aus dem Video auf. 

 

 

 

Du bist dran

  • Mache dein Projekt interaktiv. Füge eine Variable für den Winkel hinzu und verwende diese als Eingabe für den drehe Block.
    Erhöhe den Wert, der in der Variablen gespeichert ist, wenn die rechte Pfeiltaste gedrückt wird und verringere ihn mit der linken Pfeiltaste.  


    Hinweis: Um zu verhindern, dass du die Winkel-Variable von jedem Klon aus anpasst, achte darauf, sie aus dem fortlaufend-Skript zu ändern. Dieses Skript wird nur vom Vorfahr-Objekt ausgeführt.  

    Hinweis: Abhängig davon, wie schnell dein Computer ist, kann die Variable sich mehrmals mit einem Tastendruck ändern. Um das zu verhindern, kannst du warten, bis die Taste nicht mehr gedrückt ist, bevor du die weiteren Blöcke in diesem Skript ausführst.

 

  • Programmiere eine “virtuelle Limonade” Animation. Erstelle ein Blasen-Kostüm und klone dein Objekt fortlaufend.

    Jeder Klon sollte:

    • an einer zufälligen Stelle am unteren Rand der Bühne erscheinen.
    • Seine y-Position ändern, bis er eine bestimmte Position am oberen Rand der Bühne erreicht.
    • sich löschen, wenn er diesen y-Wert überschreitet.  

     Entwickle deine Animation weiter.
    Hier sind einige Ideen, die deine Limonaden-Animation realistischer und spannender machen:

    Setze die Größe der Blasen zufällig.

    Gib jeder Blase einen Farbe-Effekt.

    		 

    Füge eine kleine horizontale Bewegung (x-Achse) hinzu.

    Lasse die Blasen mit verschiedener Geschwindigkeit aufsteigen.
    Entweder zufällig oder basierend auf ihrer Größe – größere Blasen steigen schneller als kleinere.

    Ergänze eine zufällige Wartezeit, bevor die Klone gelöscht werden.

    		 

    Ändere den Hintergrund, damit er dem Farbschema deiner Blasen entspricht.

 

  • Hier findest du die Aufgaben auch zum Herunterladen:

    • als PDF:  https://dam.sap.com/mac/u/a/z6gYL2A?rc=10&doi=SAP1326859 
    • als editierbare docx Version:  https://dam.sap.com/mac/u/a/MzxBTT8?rc=10&doi=SAP1326861 

    Lösungsvorschläge für Einheit 2 - Lektion 3 findest du in dieser Collection .

 

 

Zusammenfassung - das hast du diese Lektion gelernt

Bausteine dieser Lektion: 

Erzeugt einen temporären Klon eines Objekts. Dieser Klon wird nicht im Objektbereich angezeigt. Temporäre Klone können per Block oder durch Klicken auf den roten Stopp-Knopf gelöscht werden. 
Wird einmalig ausgeführt, jedesmal wenn ein Klon erzeugt wird.
Das Skript das an diesen Startblock angefügt ist, wird nur vom Klon selbst, nicht aber dem Vorfahr-Objekt ausgeführt. 

Löscht den Klon, der diesen Block ausführt, programmatisch.

Initialisiert eine Skriptvariable, die nur innerhalb des Skriptes gültig ist, in dem sie definiert wurde. 

Skriptvariablen können durch Klick auf die orangene Variable umbenannt werden. Mit den Pfeilköpfen können weitere Skriptvariablen hinzugefügt oder entfernt werden. 

 

Klone

Das Hauptthema dieser Lektion waren Klone. Klone sind sogenannte flache Kopien des ursprünglichen Objekts.

Das bedeutet, dass sie komplexe Eigenschaften wie Skripte, Kostüme, Klänge und Variablen gemeinsam mit ihrem Vorfahr nutzen. Im biologischen Sinne teilen Klone dasselbe „genetische Material“ mit dem Organismus, von dem sie geklont wurden (in diesem Fall sind das die Skripte). Dieses „genetische Material“ kann sich jedoch aufgrund äußerer Bedingungen unterschiedlich ausprägen (in der Biologie z. B. Klima oder Ernährung, in der Informatik z. B. zufällige Eingabewerte).

Programmatisch erzeugte Klone übernehmen die Werte ihres Vorfahr-Objekts für Eigenschaften wie Größe, Kostüm, Position oder Grafikeffekte.

Du kannst den Block Wenn ich als Klon starte verwenden, um Klone zu erzeugen und zu individualisieren, sodass sie ein anderes Kostüm tragen, sich an eine andere Position bewegen oder eine andere Größe haben. Dieses Konzept wird in der Informatik als „Initialisierung“ bezeichnet.

Mit Klonen kannst du Projekte mit vielen Agenten erstellen, die sich ähnlich verhalten. Dadurch lassen sich komplexe Projekte wesentlich schneller programmieren, da du nur ein einziges Objekt modellieren musst, das du anschließend beliebig oft klonen kannst.

 

Skriptvariablen

Du hast bereits in früheren Lektionen objekt-lokale (gelten innerhalb eines Objekts) und globale Variablen (gelten im gesamten Projekt) kennengelernt. In dieser Lektion hast du eine dritte Art von Variablen kennengelernt: Skriptvariablen.

Skriptvariablen haben einen eingeschränkteren Geltungsbereich; sie sind nur innerhalb des Skripts gültig, in dem sie definiert wurden. Außerhalb dieses Skripts kann weder auf sie zugegriffen noch ihr Wert gelesen oder verändert werden.

Skriptvariablen sind besonders nützlich, wenn du sie nur in einem einzelnen Skript verwenden möchtest und ihre Werte außerhalb dieses Skripts nicht benötigt werden. In dieser Lektion haben wir sie verwendet, um die Geschwindigkeit jedes Klons in der Blume individuell festzulegen.

Einer der großen Vorteile von Skriptvariablen ist, dass sie die Blockpalette nicht überladen, da sie keinen dauerhaften Variablen-Reporter in der "Variablen"-Kategorie erhalten.

Du kannst eine Skriptvariable mit dem Block Skriptvariable _ erstellen. Wenn du auf die orangefarbene Variable klickst, öffnet sich ein Dialogfenster, in dem du die Variable umbenennen kannst. Ein Klick auf die Pfeilspitze nach rechts fügt weitere Skriptvariablen hinzu. Der Pfeil nach links löscht die letzte Skriptvariable, wenn mehr als eine vorhanden ist. 

Skriptvariablen können durch Klick auf die orange Variable umbenannt werden. Gib den gewünschten Namen im Dialogfenster ein und klicke auf "OK" um den Namen der Variablen zu ändern.

 

Manchmal ist es hilfreich, die Werte einer Variable zu beobachten, während das Programm ausgeführt wird. Da Skriptvariablen keinen Berichtsblock in der Blockpalette erhalten, kannst du das Kontrollkästchen zum Anzeigen auf der Bühne nicht aktivieren. Es gibt jedoch zwei andere Möglichkeiten, ihre Werte auf der Bühne anzuzeigen.

Du kannst entweder mit der rechten Maustaste auf das Skript klicken, das die Skriptvariable enthält, und den Variablennamen am Ende des Kontextmenüs auswählen. Beachte dabei, dass Skriptvariablen nur existieren, während das Skript ausgeführt wird, in dem sie definiert sind.

Alternativ kannst du die Blöcke zeige Variable und verstecke Variable verwenden. Wähle die Variable aus, die angezeigt werden soll. Sobald der Block ausgeführt wird, erscheint die entsprechende Variablenanzeige auf der Bühne. Um eine bestimmte Variable auszublenden, kannst du sie im Auswahlmenü des Blocks verstecke Variable auswählen. Wenn du das Eingabefeld leer lässt, werden alle temporären Variablen ausgeblendet, die derzeit auf der Bühne angezeigt werden.

Skriptvariablen und ihre Werte können auf der Bühne angezeigt werden mithilfe der zeige Variable und verstecke Variable Blöcke. Alternativ kann die Variable auch vom Kontextmenü des Skripts, in dem sie definiert ist, angezeigt werden. Klicke dafür mit der rechten Maustaste auf das Skript, während es aktiv ist, und wähle den Variablennamen am Ende des Menüs aus.

 

 

Und jetzt?

Das wars mit Einheit 2 - Lektion 2: Gerüchteküche - erste eigene Funktionen.

  • Zur nächsten Lektion des Kurses geht es hier: https://community.sap.com/t5/sap-young-thinkers-program-blog-posts/rekursion-einen-baum-wachsen-lassen-einheit-2-lektion-4/ba-p/14442831 
  • Zur Übersichtsseite von Einheit 2 geht es hier: https://community.sap.com/t5/sap-young-thinkers-program-blog-posts/einheit-2-bausteine-f%C3%BCr-syst... 
  • Zur Übersichtsseite des gesamten Kurses geht es hier: https://community.sap.com/t5/sap-young-thinkers-program-blog-posts/programmieren-mit-snap-building-u... 
  • Eine Englische Version des Kurses, bei der ein Zertifikat erworben werden kann, findet sich hier: https://learning.sap.com/courses/get-coding-with-snap-building-up-to-ai 

 

Dieses Material ist veröffentlicht unter der CC 4.0 Lizenz durch das Snap! Team bei SAP

entry.title: Advanced VizFrame Customization in SAPUI5 OVP Applications
entry.content:
Advanced VizFrame Customization in SAPUI5 OVP Applications

Introduction

As we all know, that we can create Fiori charts easily using the standard templates provided by SAP, but it does not give enough flexibility to customize on top of that, when we want to make any changes in those standard templates then we must make use of custom charts. For custom charts, VizFrames are used to perform the customization on the chart as per the requirement.

In one of my recent implementations, I defined the custom chart using VizFrame with two lines and two columns on x and y axis. Apart from this there was a dynamic colour change option given to the user, so that they can dynamically change colors of both the lines and columns, and the colour once picked, has persisted in the localStorage of the browser for next time use.

This blog explains the implementation steps:

Business Requirement

  • Dual combination chart (2lines and 2 bars)
  • Allow user to personalize chart colours.
  • Saves the colour preference.

Solution

To achieve the above requirement, we have used

  • sap.viz.ui5.controls.VizFrame
  • dual_combinatoin chart type
  • ColorPickerPopover
  • Sap.m.Menu
  • Browser localStorage

Step1: Create a dual combination chart using VizFrame:

The custom chart is created using VizFrame with dual_combination as the chart type.

<viz:VizFrame id="idVizFrame" vizType="dual_combination" uiConfig="{applicationSet:'fiori'}">
</viz:VizFrame>

Step2: Define the chart dimensions and measures:

Here, I am defining the chart dimensions and measures.

Dimension:<vizDataset:DimensionDefinition
                             name="Period"
                             value="{Date}" />

Measure:  <vizDataset:MeasureDefinition
                             name="Metric A"
                              value="{Value1}" />

                     <vizDataset:MeasureDefinition
                             name="Metric B"
                             value="{Value2}" />

                   <vizDataset:MeasureDefinition
                             name="Metric C"
                             value="{Value3}" />

                 <vizDataset:MeasureDefinition
                             name="Metric D"
                             value="{Value4}" />

Step3: Configuring chart feeds:

First, I have configured the primary axis to display column measures and then the secondary axis to display line measures.

<vizFeeds:FeedItem uid="valueAxis" type="Measure" values="Metric A,Metric B"/>

<vizFeeds:FeedItem uid="valueAxis2" type="Measure" values="Metric C,Metric D"/>

<vizFeeds:FeedItem uid="categoryAxis" type="Dimension" values="Period"/>

Step4: Creating colour palette button:

Enabling palette button as drop-down menu, so that the user can select the measure for which personalized colour needs to be picked.

<Button
       icon="sap-icon://palette"
       type="Transparent"
       tooltip="Change Chart Colors"
       press=".onOpenColorSettings"/>

When the user clicks on the menu item, a color selection menu appears.

onOpenColorSettings: function(oEvent) {
        if (!this._oColorMenu) {
            this._oColorMenu = new sap.m.Menu({
                items: [
                   new sap.m.MenuItem({ 
                   text: "Metric A",
                   press: this.onMeasureSelect.bind(this, "Metric A")   }),
                   new sap.m.MenuItem({
                   text: "Metric B",
                   press: this.onMeasureSelect.bind(this, "Metric B")  })
               ]
         });
      }
     this.oColorMenu.openBy(oEvent.getSource());
    }

As shown in the snap below, we get a colour palette button, with the dropdown menu and all the required options, and when we select on each one, we get the colour picker as shown in the snap below.

     

Step5: Define the default colours during Initialization:

I have set the default colour values for all measures inside onInit() method.

this.aColors = {
     "Metric A": "#2E86AB",
     "Metric B": "#28B463",
     "Metric C": "#F39C12",
     "Metric D": "#8E44AD"
 };

Step6: Applying personalized colours using dataPointStyle in VizFrame:

I have used VizFrame's dataPointStyle config to apply personalized colours to the measures.

plotArea: {
     dataPointStyle: {
          rules: [
            { 
              dataContext: { measureNames: "Metric A"},
               properties: {color: "#2E86AB"}
            },
           {
               dataContext: {measureNames: "Metric B"},
               properties: {color: "#28B463"}
           }
         ]

Step7: Open color picker when the measure is selected:

When the measure is selected from the menu, a color picker dialog appears.

_openColorPicker: function(oControl, sMeasure) {

        if (!this._oColorPicker) {
             this._oColorPicker = new sap.ui.unified.ColorPickerPopover({
                  colorString: "HEX",
                  change: fuction(oEvent) {
                       var sColor = oEvent.getParameter("colorString");
                        this.applyMeasureColor( sMeasure, sColor );
                  }.bind(this)
             });
           }
       this._oColorPicker.openBy(oControl);
      }

Step8: To dynamically update the VizFrame properties:

The selected color is updated dynamically by updating the VizFrame properties.

_applyMeasureColor: function(sMeasure, sColor) {
          var oVizFrame = this.byId("idVizFrame");
          var oProps = oVizFrame.getVizProperties();
          oProps.plotArea.dataPointStyle.rules =
                oProps.plotArea.dataPointStyle.rules.map(function(oRule) {
                      if (oRule.dataContext.measureNames === sMeasure) {
                               oRule.properties.color = sColor;
                        }
                   return oRule;
                });
               oVizFrame.setVizProperties(oProps);
    }

Step9: Persist the selected colours using browser local storage:

The user selected colors are stored in the browser's local storage, so that the preferred colors are picked up when you reopen the application.

localStorage.setItem(
        "CHART_COLORS",
          JSON.stringify(oColorObject)
    );

We can retrieve the same during initialization, so that the colours remain consistent even while reopening the application.
 

 JSON.parse(
        localStorage.getItem("CHART_COLORS")
   );

Output

Here is the final working chart snap:

Conclusion

This shows that instead of limiting to pre-defined charts or templates, one can personalize the charts and visualize their own preferences and in turn makes it easy to read and analyse data using VizFrame.

Reference: Documentation - Demo Kit - SAPUI5 SDK

entry.title: Moving Past the AI "Sprinkle": Context, Company Memory & the Autonomous Enterprise
entry.content:
We’ve all seen the wave of enterprise AI tools that feel like a flashy feature layered on top of legacy architecture—what JPMorgan Chase aptly calls "sprinkling AI" onto broken processes. But as SAP laid out during its recent Sapphire event, the future isn't about adding chat boxes to old screens; it’s about a fundamental shift toward an Autonomous Enterprise driven by deep context, untethered orchestration, and multi-agent workflows.

Looking closely at the evolution of Joule Studio 2.0 and SAP’s broader ecosystem partnerships, it is clear that the real value of enterprise AI lies where workflow modernization and organizational data intersect.

The Fallacy of Raw Speed vs. Contextual Accuracy

A major takeaway from recent benchmarks is that while raw code generation in Joule might occasionally take a beat longer than generic AI platforms due to deeper contextual analysis, the actual end-to-end business outcome is delivered up to 10x faster.

This makes complete sense when you look at how Anthropic's Claude models are being woven into SAP Business AI. In finance, procurement, or supply chain workflows, an AI assistant is useless if it hallucinates or lacks an understanding of your underlying data structures. By prioritizing transparency, safety, and deep context over raw, unchecked speed, the system ensures that AI-driven decisions are audit-ready and grounded in enterprise reality.

Going Deeper: Where the Ledger Meets Autonomous Execution

JPMorgan Chase’s modernization journey with SAP perfectly illustrates this philosophy. Instead of treating AI as a separate analytical layer, they are embedding SAP AI agents directly into end-to-end financial workflows.

The goal here is proactive variance detection. Imagine AI agents that catch anomalies before they ever hit the ledger, mapping historical patterns over time to pinpoint the root cause of an issue. For anyone tasked with connecting operational systems to financial databases, this is a massive paradigm shift. It moves AI from a passive tool you ask for reports into an active guardrail that keeps data pristine. Because every action is traceable within SAP’s embedded control framework, it balances speed and scale with essential enterprise trust.

Bridging the Knowledge Gap with "Company Memory"

But how do these AI agents actually know how your specific business operates? This is where the introduction of the Autonomous Suite and Company Memory (built on SAP Signavio) becomes a game-changer.

Company Memory captures the unstructured, institutional knowledge buried in policies, emails, chats, and historical operational data. It extracts what SAP calls "process atoms"—the smallest, reusable units of business knowledge.

My Perspective: This concept of "process atoms" is exactly what has been missing in standard low-code/no-code workflow automations. Instead of hardcoding endless conditional logic loops for every edge case, these atoms act as semantic guardrails. They give custom AI agents—built or extended via Joule Studio 2.0—the exact operational boundaries they need to make smart, independent decisions.

When an anomaly occurs, the agent doesn't just flag a generic error; it references Company Memory to understand why the variance happened and how it was resolved in the past, shrinking resolution times from weeks to mere minutes.

The Path Forward

SAP’s decision to position Joule Studio 2.0 as an open, vendor-agnostic platform supporting third-party models—and making these development capabilities available free until the end of the year—is a massive open door for ecosystem growth.

This shows that enterprise AI is moving past simple chatbots that just answer questions. By grounding AI agents in actual business data and Signavio’s "Company Memory," we are entering the era of the autonomous enterprise. AI is changing from a passive assistant into an active team member that securely runs and fixes workflows alongside us.

 

Reference

SAP. (2026). SAP Sapphire Virtual 2026 dashboard [Webpage]. https://www.sap.com/events/sapphire/virtual/flow/sap/sv26/dashboard/page/dashboard

entry.title: RAP Augmentation in Managed Scenario – Enriching Transactional Requests Before BO Processing
entry.content:
Introduction

The RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP) provides several extension points for implementing business logic, such as Determinations, Validations, Actions, and Prechecks. RAP Augmentation adds another extension point that allows developers to enrich incoming create or update requests before the framework starts processing the business object.

This early execution makes augmentation useful for scenarios where default values, audit information, or business-specific data must be available before any further RAP processing takes place. In this blog, we will understand RAP Augmentation, compare it with Determinations, and implement it using an Employee Management example.

Business Requirement

I had a requirement  an Employee Management application where users create and maintain employee records. During record creation, the application should automatically populate business-specific information instead of relying on users to enter every value manually.

The application should:

  • Set the employee status to PENDING_APPROVAL.
  • Determine whether approval is required based on the selected department.
  • Capture the current user as the creator.
  • Maintain audit information during updates.

Instead of implementing all this logic inside the business object, we will use RAP Augmentation to enrich the incoming request before standard processing begins.

Problem Statement

Determinations are commonly used in RAP to derive or update field values. However, they execute after the request has already entered the transactional buffer.

In some business scenarios, values must be available even before the business object starts processing. For example, default values or audit fields may be required before validations or other business logic execute. In such cases, Augmentation provides a cleaner and more suitable solution by enriching the request at the beginning of the transaction.

What is RAP Augmentation?

RAP Augmentation is an extension mechanism that runs during the Interactive Phase of a RAP transaction. It allows developers to enrich incoming create or update requests before the framework processes the business object.

Rather than modifying persisted data, augmentation focuses on preparing the request by supplying additional values or applying business-specific logic. Once the request is enriched, RAP continues with its normal processing flow.

Augmentation Flow

The request passes through augmentation before reaching the business object implementation. During this stage, additional values can be added or modified, ensuring the business object receives a complete request for further processing.

The overall execution flow is:

  • User sends a create or update request.
  • RAP invokes the registered augmentation.
  • The request is enriched with additional data.
  • Standard RAP processing continues.
  • Data is persisted to the database.

Augmentation vs Determination

Defined at the Projection Behavior Definition using use create/update (augment).Defined at the Interface Behavior Definition using  determination.
Implemented in a Projection Behavior Implementation Class Implemented in the Behavior Pool  class.
Runs during the Interactive Phase, before the BO implementation processes the request.Runs during ON MODIFY or ON SAVE, after data enters the transactional buffer.
Used to enrich or modify the incoming request before BO processing.Used to derive or recalculate values based on data in the transactional buffer.

When Execution timing matter?

The main advantage of Augmentation is when it executes. Since it runs before business object processing, any values added during augmentation are available to the rest of the RAP processing flow.

  • If the application should automatically set an employee's status or determine whether approval is required, these values are already present when the framework performs subsequent processing. If the same logic were implemented in a Determination, the values would only be available later in the transaction.

When to Use Augmentation?

Augmentation is a good choice when the incoming request needs to be enriched before RAP starts processing the business object.

Typical use cases include:

  • Setting default values.
  • Populating audit fields.
  • Deriving values from user input.
  • Performing lightweight request enrichment.
  • Preparing data required by later processing steps.

It is not intended to replace Determinations, which remain the preferred option for business logic that should execute after the request has entered the transactional buffer.

 

BDEF of Interface

managed implementation in class zbp_aky_i_emp unique;
strict ( 2 );

define behavior for zaky_i_emp //alias <alias_name>
persistent table zaky_t_emp

lock master

authorization master ( instance )
//etag master <field_name>
{
  create( authorization : global ) ;
  update;
  delete;
  action Approve result [1] $self;
  mapping for zaky_t_emp
{
  Employeeid        = employeeid;
  Firstname         = firstname;
  Lastname          = lastname;
  Department        = department;
  Status            = status;
  Approvalrequired  = approvalrequired;
  Createdby         = createdby;
  Createdat         = createdat;
  Lastchangedby     = lastchangedby;
  Lastchangedat     = lastchangedat;
  }
}

BDEF of Projection

projection implementation in class zbp_aky_c_emp unique;
strict ( 2 );

define behavior for zaky_c_emp //alias <alias_name>
{
  use create( augment );
  use update( augment );
    use delete;
  field ( readonly ) Status;
  field ( readonly ) Approvalrequired;
  field ( readonly ) Createdby;
  field ( readonly ) Createdat;
  field ( readonly ) Lastchangedby;
  field ( readonly ) Lastchangedat;
  use action Approve;
}

BP of Projection

CLASS lhc_zaky_c_emp DEFINITION INHERITING FROM cl_abap_behavior_handler.
  PRIVATE SECTION.

    METHODS augment_create FOR MODIFY
      IMPORTING entities FOR CREATE zaky_c_emp.

    METHODS augment_update FOR MODIFY
      IMPORTING entities FOR UPDATE zaky_c_emp.

ENDCLASS.

CLASS lhc_zaky_c_emp IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD augment_create.
  LOOP AT entities INTO DATA(entity).

    IF entity-Department IS INITIAL.
      APPEND VALUE #(
        %cid = entity-%cid
        %msg = new_message_with_text(
                 severity = if_abap_behv_message=>severity-error
                 text     = 'Department is mandatory' ) )
      TO reported-zaky_c_emp.        

      APPEND VALUE #( %cid = entity-%cid )
      TO failed-zaky_c_emp.          
      CONTINUE.
    ENDIF.

    MODIFY AUGMENTING ENTITIES OF zaky_i_emp  
      ENTITY zaky_i_emp
      CREATE FIELDS ( Status Approvalrequired Createdby Createdat )
      WITH VALUE #( (
        %cid             = entity-%cid
        Status           = 'PENDING_APPROVAL'
        Approvalrequired = COND #(
                             WHEN entity-Department = 'HR'
                               OR entity-Department = 'FIN'
                             THEN abap_true
                             ELSE abap_false )
        Createdby        = sy-uname
        Createdat        = cl_abap_context_info=>get_system_date(  )
      ) ).

  ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD.

METHOD augment_update.
  LOOP AT entities INTO DATA(entity).

    MODIFY AUGMENTING ENTITIES OF zaky_i_emp   
      ENTITY zaky_i_emp
      UPDATE FIELDS ( Lastchangedby Lastchangedat )
      WITH VALUE #( (
        Employeeid    = entity-Employeeid
        Lastchangedby = sy-uname
        Lastchangedat = cl_abap_context_info=>get_system_date(  )
      ) ).

  ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

BP of interface

METHOD Approve.

  " Read current status of the employee
  READ ENTITIES OF zaky_i_emp IN LOCAL MODE
    ENTITY zaky_i_emp
    FIELDS ( Status Employeeid )
    WITH CORRESPONDING #( keys )
    RESULT DATA(employees)
    FAILED DATA(read_failed).

  " Loop and update status
  LOOP AT employees INTO DATA(employee).

    " Only approve if current status is PENDING_APPROVAL
    IF employee-Status <> 'PENDING_APPROVAL'.
      APPEND VALUE #(
        %tky = employee-%tky
        %msg = new_message_with_text(
                 severity = if_abap_behv_message=>severity-error
                 text     = 'Only records with PENDING_APPROVAL status can be approved' ) )
      TO reported-zaky_i_emp.
      APPEND VALUE #( %tky = employee-%tky )
      TO failed-zaky_i_emp.
      CONTINUE.
    ENDIF.

    " Update status to APPROVED
    MODIFY ENTITIES OF zaky_i_emp IN LOCAL MODE
      ENTITY zaky_i_emp
      UPDATE FIELDS ( Status )
      WITH VALUE #( (
        %tky   = employee-%tky
        Status = 'APPROVED'
      ) )
      FAILED   DATA(update_failed)
      REPORTED DATA(update_reported).

    " Pass back result
    APPEND VALUE #(
      %tky   = employee-%tky
      %param = employee
    ) TO result.

  ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD.

Output

Click on the create 

After adding the value click on the create and put the debugger on the create-augment method to check it is triggering or not because we are doing managed application where creation is handled by framework

Execute

Currently it is in pending state click on the Approve employee.

After clicking on action

If you not adding the department

 

Conclusion

RAP Augmentation provides a simple and effective way to enrich transactional requests before the RAP framework begins processing the business object. It is particularly useful for populating default values, maintaining audit information, and preparing request data that should be available throughout the transaction.

In this example, we used augmentation to automatically populate employee details while keeping the business object implementation clean and maintainable. Understanding when to use Augmentation instead of Determinations helps developers choose the right extension point and build more maintainable RAP applications

entry.title: Trigger SAP Build Process Automation Workflow from Fiori Application
entry.content:
In this blog, we will learn how to trigger an SAP Build Process Automation (BPA) workflow directly from a SAPUI5/Fiori application.

This approach is useful when users need to start approval processes, requests, or business workflows directly from the application UI.

Prerequisites

Before starting, make sure the following things are done:

  1. Cloud Identity Services must be subscribed.
  2. SAP Build Process Automation must be subscribed.
  3. SAP Build Work Zone must be subscribed.
  4. Workflow must be deployed successfully.
  5. User must have the role: “ProcessAutomationParticipant”

Note: Workflow triggering from the UI will work after deployment because BPA runtime is accessed through destinations configured in SAP BTP.

You can find the complete implementation here: https://github.com/MaheshSirsat/trigger-bpa-workflow-from-fiori/tree/main 

Step 1 – Fetch SAP Build Process Automation Credentials

We need runtime credentials from the SAP Build Process Automation instance. These credentials will be used while creating the destination.

Navigation

  1. Open SAP BTP Cockpit.
  2. Go to Instances and Subscriptions.
  3. Locate your SAP Build Process Automation instance.

 

  1. Open the instance.
  2. Click View Credentials.

 

 

The credentials contain:

  • API endpoint URL
  • OAuth client ID
  • Client secret
  • Token URL

These details allow our Fiori application to communicate securely with the BPA runtime.

Step 2 – Create Destination

We create a destination that acts as a secure bridge between the Fiori application and the SAP Build Process Automation APIs.

Navigation

  1. Open Destinations
  2. Click Create Destination

 

 

 

Configuration

Name : sap_build_process_automation

URL : endpoints.api

Authentication : OAuth2JWTBearer

Client ID : uaa.clientid

Client Secret : uaa.clientsecret

Token Service URL : uaa.url/oauth/token

Additional Properties

 

endpoints : <copy complete endpoints object>

html5-apps-repo : <copy complete html5-apps-repo object>

saasregistryenabled : true

sap.cloud.service : com.sap.spa.processautomation

sap.cloud.service.alias : spa

The destination stores all connectivity information required to call BPA APIs. Instead of hardcoding credentials inside the application, SAP BTP securely manages them through the destination.

Step 3 – Create Fiori Application

Create a SAPUI5/Fiori application that will contain a button to start the workflow.

You can create:

  • Freestyle Application
  • List Report Application

After creation, run the application locally and ensure it opens successfully.

 

Step 4 – Configure xs-app.json

Add the following route inside xs-app.json:

{

  "source": "^/workflow/(.*)$",

  "target": "/public/workflow/rest/$1",

  "destination": "sap_build_process_automation",

  "authenticationType": "xsuaa",

  "csrfProtection": false

}

 

Whenever the application calls:

/workflow/*

the App Router automatically forwards the request to the destination configured earlier.

Step 5 – Create Workflow Trigger Button

Add a button in the UI:

<mvc:View
    controllerName="project2.controller.View1"
    xmlns:mvc="sap.ui.core.mvc"
    xmlns="sap.m">
    <Page title="Workflow Demo">
        <Button  text="Start WF"  press="onstartWF"/>
    </Page>
</mvc:View>

 

The button allows users to start the workflow directly from the UI.

Step 6 – Implement Workflow Trigger Logic

Create the following function inside the controller:

   onstartWF: async function () {
            var data = {
                definitionId: "myworkflow", // workflow-defination-id
                context: { "name": "mahesh" } // your context
            };
            await $.ajax({
                url: this._getWorkflowRuntimeBaseURL() + "/workflow-instances",
                method: "POST",
                async: false,
                contentType: "application/json",

                data: JSON.stringify(data),
                success: function (result, xhr, data) {
                    sap.m.MessageToast.show('Workflow started successfully...')
                },
                error: function (request, status, error) {
                    console.error('Error in Trigger workflow')
                }
            });
        },

 

This code sends a POST request to the BPA runtime API and creates a new workflow instance.

Understanding the Payload

{
    definitionId: "myworkflow",
    context: {        "name": "mahesh"    }
}

definitionId

definitionId: "myworkflow"

This is the workflow definition ID of the deployed workflow.

 

BPA uses this value to identify which workflow should be started.

context

context: {

    "name": "mahesh"

}

This data becomes the workflow context and can be accessed inside forms, approvals, conditions, and automation steps.

Step 7 – Generate Runtime URL

Create the helper method:

_getWorkflowRuntimeBaseURL: function () {
    var appId =this.getOwnerComponent().getManifestEntry("/sap.app/id");
    var appPath =appId.replaceAll(".", "/");
    var appModulePath = jQuery.sap.getModulePath(appPath);
    return appModulePath + "/workflow/v1";
}

 

Instead of hardcoding BPA URLs, the application uses the App Router route configured in xs-app.json.

 

Step 8 – Build and Deploy Application

Build the application:

Deploy it to SAP BTP.

 

The workflow runtime destination and App Router configuration are available only after deployment.

Step 9 – Create Catalog, Group, and Role in Work Zone

After deployment:

  1. Create Catalog
  2. Create Group
  3. Create Role
  4. Assign application tile
  5. Assign role to user

Step 10 – Test from Launchpad

  1. Open SAP Build Work Zone.
  2. Launch the application.
  3. Click Start WF.

Expected result:

Workflow started successfully...

 

You can verify the workflow instance in SAP Build Process Automation Monitoring.

Final Result

We have successfully triggered an SAP Build Process Automation workflow directly from a Fiori application.

✔ BPA credentials are retrieved from the BPA instance.

✔ Destination securely manages authentication and connectivity.

✔ xs-app.json routes requests through App Router.

✔ UI button initiates workflow execution.

✔ Workflow context can be passed dynamically from the application.

✔ Users can start workflows directly from SAP Build Work Zone.

entry.title: Counter Offers in Guided Sourcing Event
entry.content:
In a buyer’s day to day work, they submit number of Ariba events. Most of the event requires a negotiation, which also helps the buyer to build their cost saving KPI. In order to submit a counter offer, the buyer usually makes use of email communication or message board in the Ariba Sourcing event. Such negotiations are not captured in the event bid report, making it a bit of more work for the buyer. Also results in Extended Negotiation Cycles, Limited Negotiation Control, Increased Administrative Effort, Delayed Award Decisions, Reduced Transparance and Lower Process Efficiency.

In order to address this, SAP Ariba has come up with a new feature called Counter Offers in Guided Sourcing Event.

Counter Offer Process -

This feature enables buyers to propose counteroffers to suppliers during a new round of a sourcing event. A counteroffer is a special type of negotiation round in multi-round bidding, giving buyers an additional way to secure competitive pricing from suppliers. Previously, buyers could not propose a take-it-or-leave-it offer to suppliers. With this enhancement, buyers can do the following: Set a specific price as a counteroffer in a new round. Share the counteroffer with selected suppliers.

Supplier can respond to the counteroffer in the following manner: If suppliers accept, the counteroffer price is recorded as their submitted quote for that line item. Buyers can then either proceed to awarding or start another negotiation round. If there is no response from suppliers, their previously submitted quote remains valid and continues in the event.

 General Prerequisites -

Guided sourcing shall be enabled

User shall have event owner or project owner capabilities for the event.

The following event rules must be configured -

1. Enable rule “Enable counteroffer round”

2. Enable rule “Enable multi-round bidding”

3. Disable rule “Allow participants to submit bids by email”

Explained below is the step-to-step process of raising a Counteroffer round -

Step 1:  After initial round in the sourcing project, Project owner can click on the 3 dots > Event Options > Create new round to create a counter offer round as below

Step 2: Toggle the button as shown below to enable the counter offer round

Step 3: Select the counter offer type from the drop down

Step 4: Once the counter offer type is selected, click on the arrow mark to open the item details to enter counter offer value against each items

Step 5: Click on set counter offer to add final value

Step 6: Click save once the value is entered

Step 7: Repeat and add counter offer value for the required items in the sourcing event

Step 8: Select the checkbox to include all lines of the event and click done

Step 9: Click create to send the new counter offer round to supplier

Step 10: Supplier will login and open the sourcing event to revise their response, check the new counter offer value and click on accept counter offer

Step 11: Once all suppliers has submitted the response, stop the event if still the event is open

Step 12: Open the envelope to view the latest bid from the supplier

Step 13: Response can be analysed further if they have accepted the counter offer

Step 14: Click on the best bid to award the supplier with lowest bid

Conclusion

The Counteroffer Round feature enhances the negotiation process in Guided Sourcing by allowing buyers to present fixed, take it or leave it pricing to selected suppliers during a multi-round bidding event. This capability provides buyers with greater control over negotiations, helps drive more competitive pricing, and streamlines the decision-making process.

entry.title: Migrating Interfaces with Dynamic Configuration (ASMA) from SAP PO to SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
Migrating Interfaces with Dynamic Configuration (ASMA) from SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite 

This article explains how to migrate integration interfaces that rely on Dynamic Configuration (Adapter-Specific Message Attributes, ASMA) from SAP Process Orchestration (PO) to SAP Cloud Integration. It covers the technical root cause, a practical step-by-step migration approach, and the PO library dependencies involved. 

What Is ASMA (Adapter-Specific Message Attributes)? 

When running integration scenarios in SAP Process Orchestration, integration developers often need to pass dynamic, context-specific values — such as a target filename, a dynamic URL path, or a SOAP action — from the sender adapter through the integration logic to a receiver adapter. The mechanism that enables this in SAP PO is called Adapter-Specific Message Attributes (ASMA). 

ASMA values are carried in a special container called Dynamic Configuration, which travels with the message throughout the SAP PO pipeline. They can be read and written in three ways: 

  1. UDF in Message Mapping: In User-Defined Functions (UDFs) within a message mapping using the Java API: DynamicConfiguration, DynamicConfigurationKey, StreamTransformationConstants.DYNAMIC_CONFIGURATION. 
  2. Java Mapping: In a standalone Java Mapping class that extends AbstractTransformation, using input.getDynamicConfiguration() to read or write ASMA values directly on the TransformationInput object. 
  3. XSLT Mapping: Via the SAP XI Mapping API extension functions provided by the SAP XML Toolkit. These extension functions allow an XSLT stylesheet to call into the Dynamic Configuration container to read and write ASMA values at runtime. 

Core API Dependencies for Dynamic Configuration 

Dynamic Configuration API depends on two specific JAR files that are part of the Process Orchestration runtime.  

JAR File 

Key Classes Provided 

Purpose 

com.sap.xpi.ib.mapping.lib.jar 

AbstractTransformation, TransformationInput, TransformationOutput, StreamTransformationException, AbstractTrace 

Core mapping framework. All Java Mappings must extend AbstractTransformation and implement the transform() method. 

com.sap.xi.mapping.tool.lib_api.jar 

DynamicConfiguration, DynamicConfigurationKey, StreamTransformationConstants 

Dynamic Configuration API. Provides the typed container for reading and writing Adapter-Specific Message Attributes by namespace/attribute key. 

 

So, how to migrate an Interface with ASMA Using the SAP Integration Suite capability? 

1. Create a Data Extraction in Migration Assessment (https://help.sap.com/docs/integration-suite/sap-integration-suite/migration-assessment) 

Run Migration Assessment first to connect to your SAP PO system, extract all integration artifacts, and evaluate each scenario. As we will explore in this post, extracting this data reveals both the complexity and the use of Dynamic Configuration. 

Rules identified: 

  • GMMCustomUDFUsageCount 
  • GMMCustomUDFDynamicConfiguration 
  • JAVAMDynamicConfiguration 

 2. Migration Tooling (https://help.sap.com/docs/integration-suite/sap-integration-suite/migration-tooling?q=migration+tool) 

Using the assessment results as input, proceed with the Migration Tool for scenarios classified as "Ready to migrate" or "Adjustment required." The tool semi-automatically generates iFlows in SAP Integration Suite based on templates derived from the SAP PO configuration.  

3.Outcome & Validation 

After the migration, validate the migrated iFlows. The combined use of both tools ensures a structured, risk-reduced, and cost-efficient transition from SAP PO/PI to SAP Integration Suite. 

What Happens When You Migrate the Interfaces with ASMA 

Let's have a look at this demo scenario: 

 

1. Request Payload 

2. Processing Logic 

  1. Java mapping reads the input message, gets "action" and puts it into Dynamic Configuration attribute "THeaderSOAPACTION". 
  2. XSLT Mapping converts the input into message into target format and store tag "message" into Dynamic Configuration attribute "inputMessage". 
  3. Receiver Communication Channel sets the Dynamic Configuration attribute "THeaderSOAPACTION" into SOAPAction. 
  4. Once the target system processes the message, there is a Graphical Mapping with “User Defined Functions (UDF)”. This function assign values coming from Dynamic Configuration Attributes, "THeaderSOAPACTION" and "inputMessage", and puts into the tags "dynAction" and "inputMessage" respectivelly. 

 3. Response Payload 

 

Once the interface is migrated, you will find in your Integration Suite package the artifacts generated based on your SAP PO objects. 

Check the Interface (flow). You will see boxes representing the logic that was defined on your SAP PO with wrapper functions depending on your ASMA use case.  

 

JAVA MAPPING (Using Dynamic Configuration) – A wrapper class is generated by the Migration tool by default to provide temporary compatibility. While you can override the Java method to retain Dynamic Configuration logic, this approach is not recommended for production use. The preferred approach is to replace the Dynamic Configuration calls with message headers as described in the adaptation section below. 

 XSLT MAPPING (Using Dynamic Configuration) - The migrated XSLT artifact retains the SAP XI Mapping API calls. These must be adapted to use Cloud Integration-native header access mechanisms. 

GRAPHICAL MAPPING (Using Dynamic Configuration) - UDFs referencing the Dynamic Configuration API are migrated as-is and will require manual adaptation. 

 

 

How to Adapt ASMA-Based Interfaces for Cloud Integration 

Evaluate your scenario — Identify all ASMA usages across Java Mappings, UDFs, and XSLT stylesheets. The Migration tool does not replace Dynamic Configuration logic. 

Understand how messages are processed by Cloud Integration. "Dynamic Values” are passed via named message headers or properties (set by a Content Modifier or Groovy Script step) not via a Dynamic Configuration container. 

 

 In SAP Cloud Integration, the correct mechanism for passing dynamic values through an integration flow is via message headers or exchange properties. These are set in dedicated flow steps before the mapping executes and then referenced in the mapping logic or adapter configuration. 

 

Concept 

SAP PO (Examples) 

SAP Cloud Integration (Examples) 

Dynamic value container 

  • Dynamic Configuration (ASMA) 
  • Message Headers 
  • Exchange Properties 

How values are set 

  • Java DynamicConfiguration.put() 
  • Content Modifier 
  • Groovy Script setProperty() / setHeaders() 

How values are read in mapping 

  • Java DynamicConfiguration.get() 
  • Content Modifier 
  • Graphical Mapping out-of-the-box functions to getProperty and getHeader 
  • Groovy Script getProperty() / getHeaders() 

Dynamic adapter config 

  • Adapter reads ASMA by namespace + attribute key 
  • Adapter uses expression ${header.HEADER_NAME} or ${property.PROPERTY_NAME} 

 

Therefore, if an imported mapping uses Dynamic Configuration APIs for accessing Adapter-Specific Message Attributes, the integration flow must be manually adjusted to ensure correct execution.  

 

References 

Links 

Creating Message Mapping as an Artifact 

https://help.sap.com/docs/integration-suite/sap-integration-suite/developing-message-mapping-as-artifact?locale=en-US 

Headers and Exchange Properties Provided by the Integration Framework 

https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-integration/sap-cloud-integration/headers-and-exchange-properties-provided-by-integration-framework 

Access Headers and Properties in Scripts 

https://help.sap.com/docs/integration-suite/sap-integration-suite/access-headers-and-properties-in-scripts 

 

 

Let's Review the Integration Flow Imported in the Previous Steps and Look into Adaptations 

 

 1. Java Mapping Replacement: A Content Modifier step is added before the mapping to set the SOAPAction message header and store the inputMessage value into message property. This replaces the Dynamic Configuration to write operations previously performed in Java Mapping. 

 

 2. XSLT Mapping: Remove Dynamic Configuration functionalities 

 

 3. Graphical Mapping: UDFs adapted to use out-of-the-box functions instead of the Dynamic Configuration API. 

 

 

 

 Additionally, for XI Adapter, Natively Supports ASMA 

The XI adapter in Cloud Integration natively supports Adapter-Specific Message Attributes. The XI sender adapter automatically creates a pair of exchange properties for each ASMA: one holding the value and one holding the namespace. When configuring ASMAs for the XI receiver adapter, both properties must be explicitly created. 

For scenarios using the XI adapter, the migration of ASMA-based logic can therefore be automated. For other adapter types, a similar approach applies, with the key distinction that the namespace property is typically not required. It is specifically needed by the XI receiver adapter, but not by other adapters. 

In pipeline-based integration approaches this is handled automatically within the generic XI inbound and XI outbound flows. Since exchange properties are not passed between flows, corresponding headers are created at the XI inbound step and then converted back to properties at the XI outbound step. 

For further details, see: 

References 

Links 

Generic Inbound and Outbound 

Generic Inbound and Outbound | SAP Help Portal 

 

Summary 

Migrating integration scenarios that use Dynamic Configuration (ASMA) from SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite requires a multi-step approach. 

The recommended migration path combines two capabilities of SAP Integration Suite: Migration Assessment, to identify all affected integration scenarios and quantify ASMA usage through rules such as GMMCustomUDFDynamicConfiguratiom, GMMCustomUDFUsageCount and JAVAMDynamicConfiguration; and the Migration Tool, to semi-automatically generate iFlows from existing SAP PO artifacts. 

However, automated migration alone may not be sufficient. Depending on how Dynamic Configuration was used, each mapping type may requires targeted manual adaptation. 

By combining the structured assessment and tooling capabilities with these targeted adaptations, teams can achieve a controlled, risk-reduced migration from SAP PO/PI to SAP Integration Suite while preserving the intended runtime behavior of each interface.

entry.title: Re: A Step-by-Step Guide to Integrating SAP SuccessFactors with Power BI through Direct OData Feed
entry.content:
Hello @Berg_Song 
we followed the guide and the prerequisite to generate the token in IAS, but looks like we're stuck and the token is not being consumed in SuccessFactors.

The following has been successfully validated:

  • ✅ IAS authentication is successful.
  • ✅ ID Token (JWT) is successfully generated.
  • ✅ JWT Bearer token exchange completes successfully.
  • ✅ Bearer Access Token is successfully returned.
  • ✅ IAS Sender Application (Power BI) is configured.
  • ✅ Dependency configured as Power_BI with API sf_technical_access.
  • ✅ SuccessFactors receiver is configured as the SAP-managed Hybrid SAML + OpenID Connect application.
  • ✅ SuccessFactors Application Map contains the correct Client ID.
  • ✅ Bind To User is enabled and mapped to right user.
  • ✅ The user is active and has the required API/RBP permissions.
  • ✅ Subject Name Identifier is configured as Login Name and the ID token now contains sub = SF Username (IAS login name).
  • ✅ Preview tenant and API endpoint (https://api2preview.sapsf.eu) have been verified.

Despite the above, every call to the SuccessFactors OData API (including the OData service root /odata/v2/) returns:

  • ❌ HTTP 401
  • ❌ LGN0007 – Invalid OAuth token

Are we doing anything wrong?
We get the token from IAS so we are not following the steps above to get the token from SF, but rather we are trying to make the Odata call with the token from IAS.
Thanks
Marco

entry.title: Re: Inquiry on Creating or Renaming Bank Account Types in...
entry.content:
Dear @hyik,

Account Types cannot be created or maintained directly in the Manage Bank Accounts app. However a new customer-specific Account Type can be created through the relevant bank account master data configuration, provided that Advanced Bank Account Management (scope item J77) is active and SSCUI 102336 – Define Settings for Bank Account Master Data is available

 

SAP does not provide a documented standard procedure for renaming SAP-delivered Account Types, for example from 01 – Current Account to 01 – Current Account TEST. Therefore the standard descriptions should generally be retained and a new customer-specific Account Type should be created instead of changing the meaning of an existing SAP-delivered entry

Please verify the technical editability of the existing descriptions in a system where J77 and SSCUI 102336 are active. J77 is not active in the system available for testing, so I cannot confirm with complete certainty whether the description of an existing Account Type can technically be changed in this configuration activity

More details below:

SAP Help – Bank Account Types and Contract Types

SAP KBA 3043922 – Error message no. /FTI/WEBGUI_MSG139 occurs in SSCUI Define Settings for Bank Account Master Data

SAP KBA 3130880 – Message ID SV052 for SSCUI Define Settings for Bank Account Master Data

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Banking Payments paid Invoices posting dates of Invoi...
entry.content:
Hi,

If you need the G/L Account Number from the A/R Invoice lines, you can join the INV1 table to OINV using the invoice DocEntry and the invoice line number (RCT2.DocLine).

LEFT JOIN INV1 T5
ON T5.DocEntry = T2.DocEntry
AND T5.LineNum = T1.DocLine

Then simply select the account:

T5.AcctCode AS 'GL Account'

I tested it in my environment, and it returned the correct G/L account from the invoice.

Best Regards .

entry.title: Onboarding 2.0 Limitation for Sweden to US Transfer/Rehire Scenario
entry.content:
Hello All,

We are looking for SAP's recommended approach and best practices for the following scenario.

Scenario

We have an existing employee transferring from a Sweden Legal Entity to a US Legal Entity.

The employee already has an active employment record in Sweden with a future termination date, and a future-dated US employment is being created. However, when processing this scenario through Onboarding 2.0, the candidate gets stuck at the Perform Rehire Verification step.

The challenge is that the system does not provide an option to proceed with a rehire using the existing (old) employment record because the employee still has an active/future-dated employment relationship in another country. As a result, we are unable to move forward with the onboarding process and generate the required US compliance activities.

This becomes particularly critical because employees joining a US legal entity must complete Form I‑9 Section 1 and Section 2, which are normally generated as part of the Onboarding 2.0 compliance process.

Questions

  1. What is SAP's recommended and supported approach for handling this type of cross-country transfer/rehire scenario?

  2. Has anyone successfully implemented a workaround for employees transferring from a non-US legal entity to a US legal entity where the US employment is future dated?

  3. How can the employee complete Form I‑9 Section 1 and Section 2 digitally if the standard Onboarding 2.0 compliance process cannot be generated due to this limitation?

  4. Does SAP recommend any supported workaround such as:

    • Crossboarding/Internal Hire
    • A separate onboarding process
    • Manual compliance process initiation
    • Integration with a third-party I‑9 provider
    • Any other supported solution

  5. Are there any roadmap items or planned enhancements to better support this type of transfer/rehire scenario in Onboarding 2.0?

User Experience Concern

We have also noticed a significant usability gap compared to Onboarding 1.0.

In Onboarding 1.0, administrators could easily reassign ongoing compliance tasks to another responsible user when needed.

In Onboarding 2.0, if a compliance task is assigned incorrectly or the responsible user changes, the available options often involve:

  • Cancelling the compliance process
  • Restarting onboarding
  • Re-initiating activities manually

These workarounds are time-consuming and can negatively impact both administrators and business users.

Additional Questions

  1. Is there a supported method to reassign active I‑9 or compliance tasks without cancelling or restarting the onboarding process?

  2. How are other customers handling situations where the originally assigned compliance owner is unavailable or incorrect?

  3. Are there any planned enhancements to improve compliance task reassignment and overall administrative flexibility in Onboarding 2.0?

We would greatly appreciate any guidance from SAP experts or customers who have encountered and successfully resolved a similar Sweden-to-US transfer scenario.

Thank you in advance for your support and recommendations.

SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding  @NathanWilkinson @ThomasBilbaugh 
@Gopinath_Murugaiyan

entry.title: Issue with PDF export in SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) Stories
entry.content:
When I export my SAC Story to PDF, cells containing long text (paragraph-level content) get truncated. Only a portion of the text visible on screen is included in the PDF — the remaining text is lost, even though the underlying data is complete.

I have a Case Activity Log table with columns including:

  • Activity Date
  • Category
  • Type
  • Description
  • Notes ← This column contains multi-paragraph text (medical case notes, RTW forms, patient history, etc.)

What I've Tried

1. Table Widget Styling

  •  Enabled Wrap Text in table cells
  •  Set Row Height to Auto
  • Increased Notes column width to maximum (500+ px)
  •  Reduced font size to 10
  • Result: Table still truncates text visually; PDF still cuts off content

Workaround Attempted: Excel → Word → PDF

  • Exported to XLSX via SAC
  • Opened in Excel → Applied Wrap Text + borders manually
  • Copy-pasted to MS Word (Landscape + A4 + Narrow margins)
  • Saved as PDF from Word
  • Result:  This gives complete content, but it's a manual multi-step process (not user-friendly for business users)

User need to print the report with one click export to pdf without losing data content and human readable font on letter or legal pages.

Just to get more information i made the story as canvas and it still continues to have the issue and cannot print the whole context 

 

Any of the following would be greatly appreciated:

  •  Official SAP documentation on this behavior
  • Native SAC solution or workaround
  •  Best practice for handling large text fields in SAC reports
  •  Confirmation if this is a known limitation
  • Roadmap information for potential improvements

Thank you in advance for any insights!

entry.title: AI is Transforming Support: Navigating the Autonomous Enterprise
entry.content:
At SAP Sapphire 2026, SAP introduced its vision for the Autonomous Enterprise—a future where AI agents are embedded into business processes, helping organizations operate with greater speed, intelligence, and resilience. But what does this vision mean for Technical Support?

In the latest episode of the SAP Change Makers Podcast, Oliver Huschke speaks with Uwe Wieditz, VP and Global Head of Private Cloud ERP Technical Support, and Bernhard Luecke, Senior Director, Technical Support in Private Cloud ERP, about how AI is transforming SAP Support from a reactive problem-solving function into a proactive, predictive, and increasingly autonomous operation.

Their discussion explores how agentic AI, multi-agent orchestration, and human expertise are coming together to deliver faster resolutions, improved system stability, and a better customer experience.

 “The Autonomous Enterprise vision means AI agents are becoming operators embedded directly into enterprise processes. Thinking about this concept in the support context, it means we are shifting from reactive case handling to proactive, predictive, and increasingly autonomous support operations.”
— Uwe Wieditz

“The traditional case model is being redesigned. In the Autonomous Enterprise model, many cases will never need to be created at all: Step-by-step, AI agents detect, diagnose, and resolve issues autonomously. For cases that still need human involvement, AI simplifies the experience dramatically. Customers describe their problem in natural language; agents classify, prioritize, and route instantly. Engineers receive a pre-analyzed case with suggested resolutions — not a blank ticket."

— Bernhard Luecke

Key Takeaways

  •   Better outcomes for customers. AI-powered support helps increase system stability, resolve issues in minutes, and accelerate customers’ journey toward the Autonomous Enterprise.
  •   The evolution of the support engineer. Support engineers are moving from reactive problem solvers to AI agent orchestrators, focusing on complex challenges while bringing empathy and human connection to customer interactions.
  •   Trust comes first. Security, compliance, and responsible AI governance remain non-negotiable foundations of SAP’s AI-powered support strategy.

🎧Listen to the Full Episode

Want to learn how SAP is bringing the Autonomous Enterprise vision to life through AI-powered Technical Support?

Listen to the full SAP Change Makers Podcast episode and discover how AI and human expertise are working together to redefine the future of support.

👉Podcast links

entry.title: Re: Managing Invoices with SAP Ariba Central Invoice Management (now SAP Ariba Invoicing)
entry.content:
SAP Ariba Invoicing (CIM) & Non-SAP ERP

Hello @Debadri,

Thanks for reaching out. Here are answers to your queries based on my understanding. It could be better if you connect with SAP representative for further clarifications.
For product details, refer this page from sap.com - SAP Ariba Invoicing. For contact details, scroll down to the bottom of this page.

Based on your query, we assume that you have Ariba B&I running with non-SAP ERP and are evaluating SAP Ariba Invoicing (CIM) for invoice management in the overall landscape.

Can CIM capture and process PO-based paper invoices through OCR?
Yes. This is one of the main features of the solution.

Will OCR extract the PO number from the invoice and transfer it to Ariba B&I?
CIM OCR does extract the PO number from invoice image.
CIM is architecturally designed to integrate with SAP S/4HANA Cloud as the connected ERP backend — not directly with SAP Ariba Buying & Invoicing (B&I).
The PO-based invoice flow in CIM posts invoices into the connected S/4HANA system, not into Ariba B&I.
If your landscape using Ariba B&I, the integration architecture and PO reference passing mechanism would differ from the standard CIM ↔ S/4HANA flow.

If not, is there a standard mechanism to pass PO and Goods Receipt (GR) references between Ariba B&I and CIM for invoice validation?
Not via standard CIM flow.

For invoices captured via CIM, where does 2-way/3-way matching and invoice reconciliation occur — within CIM or in Ariba Buying & Invoicing (B&I) after the invoice is transferred?
For the CIM ↔ S/4HANA standard integration: Matching and reconciliation occur within CIM, orchestrated against data retrieved from the connected S/4HANA backend.

Is there API based connectivity option available for Ariba Invoice Management with 3rd party ERP system?
Yes, API-based connectivity is available for SAP Ariba Invoicing (CIM) with third-party or non-SAP ERP systems.

  • Supplier Invoices with Structured Data API
    Allows creation of invoices in CIM via structured data payload (without OCR), including PO and GR object references at line-item level, enabling integration with third-party systems that can generate structured invoice data.
  • Enrich Supplier Invoices Externally API
    Allows to replace the default Document Information Extraction (OCR) service with a custom OCR and information extraction service. This enables third-party OCR engines to be plugged into the CIM invoice capture pipeline.

For all the APIs around SAP Ariba Invoicing, kindly refer this page:

  • SAP Business Accelerator Hub (APIs) - SAP Ariba Invoicing.

entry.title: Employee Central Data Migration: Notes from the Field, Alongside SAP's Standard Guide
entry.content:
I have been working on the SAP SF Employee Central module for the last ten years, and in every implementation project I have done, there is always an Employee Master Data migration. The Standard SAP SF Data Migration guide (IDP) is thorough, but at least six data sets demand more field judgment than any guide alone can give. Think of this blog as the practical companion to that guide, not a replacement, from a consultant who has run these templates more times than I can count.

Check Point:-

Align with the client on Employee ID range and sequence before starting upload, especially on multi-country implementations creating new SF IDs for legacy employees. Get this agreed first; once an ID is created, it is very difficult to purge and recreate.

 

1) Basic Import (UDF)

The first template to upload, and where consultants waste the most time filling every column, mandatory or not.

 

Recommendation 1: Per the standard guide, you only need Status, User ID, Username, First Name, and Last Name. Fields like Manager ID, HR ID, Title, phone, location, and email get updated automatically from later templates via HRIS Sync, so omit them here; the Sync run overrides this data anyway even if you do fill it in.

 

Exception: if EC is going live into a landscape where other SuccessFactors modules are already active, do not load the UDF first, use it only for employees hired after the last UDF import, and skip Manager/HR fields if modules like Performance already have live forms running, an incorrect manager can shift those forms unnecessarily.

 

2) Position

Major concerns I have always seen when creating position data for migration.

a ) What is the right date for creating positions?

 

Recommendation 2: Positions can practically be created with any backdated date, e.g. 01/01/1900 or 01/01/2000, based on the age of the legacy data. My preference is to date position creation similar to the employee's hire date (regular position, FTE 1), which keeps the position's “age” accurate for later reporting.

 

b) How do you map the manager's position at creation?

 

Recommendation 3: I'd suggest not mapping it at creation, since your current manager may not have been the manager at hire. Instead, map it at your cutoff date (e.g. 1st Jul 26) as a second position entry.

The standard guide also documents a configuration-driven alternative, using Position Management settings (Adapt Hierarchy During Position Import, Adapt Hierarchy in Job Info Import) so the system assigns the correct manager automatically. Either works; I prefer the manual route for the added control.

 

Recommendation 4: Be cautious of cyclic dependency when mapping manager ID. E.g. if A reports to B, and B reports to C, there is no way C can end up reporting to A.

 

c)How do you upload position data with minimum effort?

 

Recommendation 5: Reverse engineer instead of iterating. Since Position is an MDF object, create a few via the UI with different field/picklist combinations, export them, and your template with sample data is ready.

 

3) Job Information

The most difficult template of employee data migration.

 

Recommendation 6: Align with the customer to upload only two records, Hire and Go-Live, pushing remaining history into Internal Job History instead.

One caveat: if a Compensation Merit Cycle or Variable Pay is in scope for the go-live year, the customer needs at least one year of Job, Compensation, and Personal Information history; two records alone won't support the merit process.

 

Recommendation 7: The Hire record should carry NO_MANAGER, for the same reason as position creation, with minimal fields: ID, event reason, event date, legal entity, and position number. Add legacy org/job data only if the customer already has it in correct format.

 

Recommendation 8: The Go-Live/Latest record is where you should target filling all fields accurately, including Manager ID, grades, title, and org information.

Make sure Legal Entity, Business Unit, Division, Department, etc. carry the correct codes with correct association, wrong association is one of the most common causes of errors, even when codes themselves are right.

 

Recommendation 9: Turn off Workflow, Event Derivation, and Cross Portlet Rules on Job/Compensation during migration, these trigger unwanted updates on import.

Rules that simply default Job/Org attributes from Position can stay on if it aids data quality, provided each batch stays under about two hours.

 

 

4) Dependents

Second most complex object after Job Information. Here is how we can crack this nut.

 

Recommendation 10: Create a unique Dependent ID by concatenating Employee ID with a dependent sequence, e.g. employee 10000000 with four dependents gets 1000000001 through 1000000004.

Upload these with DOB under Biographical Information first, then Personal Information, National ID, etc. sequentially.

Each dependent needs at least one non-effective-dated record for the setup to be valid.

Split the template into smaller pieces and upload separately.

 

5) Recurring Deductions

Unique among SF objects, being a parent-child object.

 

Recommendation 11: First create deduction data using the Recurring Deduction template with User ID and effective date. Once this parent object is uploaded, then upload the child, Recurring Deduction Items.

Splitting the problem this way makes it far easier; a combined deduction file will keep failing and leave you exhausted.

Reverse engineer here too: map deductions for a few employees via the UI, export them, and use that to understand how end dates should be structured.

 

6) Bank/Payment Information

A similar parent-child pattern. Bank data is the parent, employee Bank Details the child.

 

Recommendation 12: Create Bank data first, with country, routing number, and Business Identifier Code as the foundation. Then upload employee Bank Details with a hire date matching the routing number, BIC, and payment method as Bank Transfer, otherwise it will keep failing. Same advice: reverse engineer bank details too.

 

Conclusion:-

There can be other challenging issues with data upload, but based on my experience, complex objects like Position, Job Information, Dependents, and Bank/Deductions can mostly be solved with 1) the recommendations above, 2) breaking templates into smaller pieces, and 3) reverse engineering.

This is what I have learnt so far, alongside the official guide, not instead of it. What data points do you find challenging, and what has worked for you?

Thanks for reading this article!

Mohammad Saddam Aquib
