entry.title: Understanding Maintenance Event Builder (MEB) Through an Aerospace Maintenance Scenario
entry.content:
INTRODUCTION

SAP Plant Maintenance provides the Maintenance Event Builder to help planners group related maintenance activities under a single maintenance event. MEB acts as a planning workbench that connects notifications, revisions, and task lists into a coordinated maintenance package.

When I first heard about Maintenance Event Builder (MEB) in SAP PM, I assumed it was just another screen to group notifications. After exploring it in the system creating revisions, assigning notifications, testing order creation in WPS1, and trying hierarchical task lists in WPSTLH, I understood that MEB is much more than that. It is a planning workbench that helps bring multiple maintenance requirements together under one event, so they can be executed in a controlled, coordinated way.

In this blog, I've used an Aerospace and Defense style example, because revision-based maintenance planning fits naturally into that kind of environment, where multiple issues on the same aircraft often need to be grouped into a single maintenance window.

Understanding Maintenance Event Builder

The first thing that became clear to me is that MEB is not where maintenance work originates. The work begins with notifications. The typical flow is:

  1. Generate a maintenance notification or list of notifications
  2. Create or select a revision
  3. Assign notifications to the revision
  4. Create orders from task lists and revision data
  5. Optionally assign to project WBS/network and schedule

Business Scenario

To make the learning practical, I imagined a one-week scheduled maintenance event in an aerospace maintenance hangar. A defense aircraft was planned for a major ground maintenance window from June 1 to June 10. During pre-inspection, several technical issues were identified across different aircraft systems. Instead of planning each one independently, the maintenance planner wanted to bundle them into a single event.

The maintenance requirements in this example are:

  • Hydraulic pump abnormal vibration.
  • Landing gear actuator seal leakage.
  • Avionics cooling fan abnormal noise.
  • Fuel pressure sensor calibration drift.
  • Cargo door locking mechanism inspection.

In a real organization, each of these could be raised as a separate notification but all of them belong to the same aircraft maintenance window. That is exactly the kind of planning problem MEB is built to solve.

Prerequisites for MEB

Before testing MEB, a few prerequisites became obvious. Without these, the process doesn't make much sense in the system.

1. Notifications must exist first MEB works on the basis of maintenance notifications. If there are no notifications, there is nothing meaningful to collect into a revision. In the scenario presented here, notifications are created first and then grouped into a revision.

2. A revision must be created A revision is created using transaction IWR1. For this example, I created a revision for June, since the maintenance window runs from June 1 to June 10

3. Hierarchical task lists should be created Hierarchical task lists are optional but useful when multiple standard maintenance procedures need to be organized under a common work package. If the end goal is to create standardized orders, the task lists need to already be in place. MEB creates orders using task lists, not from scratch.

This was one of the most important learning points in my testing. I initially assumed operations could be entered directly in WPSTLH1, but that's not how it works. Hierarchical task lists are built as a hierarchy of nodes, and operative task lists are inserted under those nodes, it's the operative task lists that contain the actual operations. In other words, WPSTLH1 is not where detailed operations are typed in one by one. It's where the hierarchy structure is built by inserting existing task lists under parent nodes.

Preparing Hierarchical Task Lists

For this aerospace example, I imagined five standard task lists with operations already maintained:

  • Hydraulic pump inspection and overhaul.
  • Landing gear actuator seal replacement.
  • Avionics cooling fan inspection.
  • Fuel pressure sensor calibration.
  • Cargo door lock mechanism inspection and functional test.

Once these standard task lists existed, the next question was whether they should sit independently or be brought together under one hierarchy. The hierarchy in WPSTLH1 ended up looking like this:

  • Parent hierarchy: Aircraft Check Work Package.
  • Child task list 1: Hydraulic pump overhaul.
  • Child task list 2: Landing gear actuator work.
  • Child task list 3: Avionics cooling fan check.
  • Child task list 4: Fuel sensor calibration
  • Child task list 5: Cargo door locking inspection.

This helped me understand the role of the hierarchy. The hierarchy does not replace the standard task lists. It organizes them under one maintenance structure.

Step-by-step Workflow

Looking at this as a business story rather than just a sequence of transactions made the process much clearer.

Step 1: Create the maintenance notification I created a notification for the "Aircraft maintenance work package". In my test, I linked the relevant task list references to the notifications where my system configuration allowed it. MEB then used that hierarchical task lists structure during order creation.

Step 2: Create the revision. Using IWR1, I created a revision for June, aligned with the June 1–10 maintenance window.

Step 3: Open MEB in WPS1 In WPS1, I selected the notification and revision created in the previous steps.

Step 4: Assign the notification to the revision. I dragged the notification number to the revision to assign it.

Step 5: Create orders Clicking "Create Orders" generates maintenance orders from the assigned notifications and task lists. Once created, click Save. Depending on the MEB configuration and order generation settings, the system may create one or multiple maintenance orders from the assigned notifications and task lists. If required, these orders can also be assigned to projects.

Common Mistakes

  • Don't expect WPSTLH1 to behave like a standard task list maintenance screen for entering operations directly.
  • Don't start with the revision and forget the notifications,MEB begins with the notification.
  • Treat the Revision as the planning container
  • Don't build a hierarchy before creating the operative task lists that contain the real operations.

Conclusion

What finally made MEB clear to me was seeing it as a planning bridge between identified maintenance requirements and executable maintenance orders. The notifications capture the maintenance requirements, the revision creates the event, the task lists define the standard work, and MEB brings all of these together into a coordinated maintenance package from which executable maintenance orders can be generated. This helps maintenance planners schedule and execute multiple activities during a common maintenance window in a controlled and structured manner.

Note: This scenario is illustrative, built to demonstrate the MEB workflow conceptually rather than reflecting an actual aircraft maintenance program.

entry.title: SI check resolution for PP-MRP
entry.content:
Hello All,

We are performing brownfield conversion from ECC to S4 HANA.  By considering business impact (e.g Once MRP area assigned to material master user need to check MRP excluded stock by entering MRP area) we are following below pre-conversion activity in different phases.

Phase 1 : Initial SI error check ( Before BASIS SUM phase)

  • Execution of RMMDVM10 (consistency check) and RMMDVM20 (rebuild planning file entries)
  • Conversion of planning file entries using RMDBVM00 / OM0F

Result: inconsistency will remove and table records transferred from MDVM to DBVM. records get updated in new table DBVM and MRP also consider these records for planning. Plant level MRP area has been created 

Phase2: Development lock phase (During Basis SUM phase)

  • MRP area configuration like MRP area activation and MRP area's TR movement to Target system.

Result: MRP area gets activated but no use of MRP areas as not assigned to any material. MRP still consider stock exclusion indicator 1 to exclude stock from MRP for that particular storage location.

Phase 3: Pre- processing phase (Pre-downtime SI check_)

Executing the program MRP_AREA_STORAGE_LOC_MIGRATION.

Result: MRP area get assign to material master and simultaneously program will remove indicator "1" . Now from here user need to enter MRP area in MD04 till downtime and MRP also will consider MRP areas for stock exclusion.

Questions: 

  1. Is there any other functional impact is we go with above mentioned phases.
  2. Is there any challenges in BASIS at the time downtime because at the time of initial SUM, table MDMA count is 0 and after execution of the table count has been increased to 12M as part of SI check resolution.

Please support here as planning to perform these steps in S4 HANA conversion.

Thanks for support in advanced.

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud – Engineering Change Management (ECM) Workflow for BOM Approval and Mass BO
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

We are currently implementing SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud and are evaluating the standard Engineering Change Management (ECM) process for Bill of Material (BOM) changes.

Our customer has the following business requirement, and we would like to understand the best practice in SAP Public Cloud.

Current Business Requirement

  • Any change to a BOM should go through only one approver.
  • The approver should be able to review the actual BOM changes (for example, component replacement, quantity change, addition/deletion of components) before approving.
  • Once approved, the changes should become effective on the defined effective date.

Additionally, the customer frequently performs mass BOM changes (similar to transaction CS20 in SAP ECC/On-Premise), where a single component is replaced or updated across multiple BOMs.

Observation in Standard Change Record Process

While testing the standard Change Record process in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud (Discrete Industry Change), we observed the following workflow:

  1. Create Change Record.
  2. Submit Change Request.
  3. Approval.
  4. Start Implementation.
  5. Change Number is generated.
  6. Implement BOM changes.
  7. Close Implementation.

Based on this flow, it appears that the Change Number is generated only after the approval and Start Implementation step.

This means the engineer cannot modify the BOM under the Change Number before the approver has approved the request.

Our Questions

  1. Is this the standard behavior in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud?
  2. If yes, does the approver only approve the engineering request rather than the actual BOM modifications?
  3. Is there any standard process that allows the approver to review the proposed BOM changes before approval?
  4. Is it possible to configure the workflow so that:
    • the Change Number is available before approval,
    • the engineer prepares the BOM changes,
    • and the approver reviews the modified BOM before approving and releasing it?
  5. If this is not supported in the standard Public Cloud process, what is the SAP-recommended approach for customers who require approval of the actual engineering changes rather than only the request?

Mass BOM Changes

The customer also performs mass BOM changes similar to CS20 in SAP ECC.

We understand that CS20 is not available in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud.

Our questions are:

  1. What is the SAP-recommended approach for mass BOM updates in Public Cloud?
  2. Should the process be:
    • Create Change Record
    • Approval
    • Execute Mass Maintenance
    • Release
  3. Or is there another recommended workflow that maintains engineering traceability and approval?
  4. Which standard Fiori apps should be used together with ECM for controlled mass BOM changes?

We would appreciate guidance on the SAP best practice for implementing this requirement while staying as close as possible to the standard Public Cloud functionality.

entry.title: Re: Order To cash Dashboard Profit Center filter not avai...
entry.content:
Dear @subraya_joshi87ec66,

The Order to Cash Dashboard uses several analytical models because its widgets cover different parts of the process:

  • C_SalesAnalyticsQry_1 – sales volume and open sales based on sales orders, deliveries and billing documents

  • C_SalesVolumeAnalyticsQry – billing-based sales volume, sales costs and profit margin

  • C_SalesOrderQuery – incoming sales orders

  • C_ServiceOrderQuery – incoming and open service orders

The selected model determines which filter fields are available. Therefore, Profit Center can be selected from the sales-volume models, while Fiscal Year is available in C_SalesOrderQuery

These filters are model-specific. They affect widgets using the selected model and affect other models only when the relevant dimensions are linked. Adding Fiscal Year therefore does not automatically replace Calendar Year or Calendar Quarter in all dashboard charts. To display the charts by fiscal periods, copy the standard story and replace the calendar time dimensions in the relevant charts with the fiscal ones

More details below:

SAP Help – Order to Cash Dashboard

SAP Help – Sales Volume and Open Sales – Query (C_SalesAnalyticsQry_1)

SAP Help – Sales Volume – Query (C_SalesVolumeAnalyticsQry)

SAP Help – Sales Order – Query (C_SalesOrderQuery)

SAP Help – Service Order – Query (C_ServiceOrderQuery)

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: What's New in Review Booklets and MDA in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2608
entry.content:
Data-driven decision-making depends on having the right insights at the right time, presented in a way that's easy to analyze and share. With the latest enhancements for Review Booklets and Multidimensional Analysis (MDA) in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, users and designers gain powerful new capabilities to explore data, customize visualizations, streamline filtering, and create more flexible reporting experiences.

From new calculation options and number filters to advanced chart settings, enhanced app state handling, and major improvements in the Review Booklet Designer, these innovations help business users work more efficiently while enabling authors to build richer and more dynamic analytical content. Let's take a closer look at what's new.

What's New in Review Booklets and MDA

Calculations

In review booklet and MDA apps, you can apply calculation logic directly on measures using a new Calculations context menu and decide how the result or value of a measure is displayed. The context menu offers various options in two submenus that are called Display Values As and Display Totals As.

For more information, see Calculations and Using Calculations.

Number Filters

Review booklet and MDA apps now include a Number Filters panel and a context menu. You can use number filters to filter and rank numeric values based on your own rules.

To define number filters, open the Number Filters dialog using either the Number Filter option in the context menu or the Number Filters panel in the side panel. Select the context and measure, then set the condition: either numeric or ranking (for example, top or bottom N values). For more information, see Number Filters and Working with Number Filters.

 

Figure 1: Number Filters dialog

Chart Settings Side Panel

You can use a new Chart Settings panel in review booklet apps to configure charts the same way as in multidimensional analysis apps. The panel enables on-the-fly switching between table and chart views and provides advanced chart configuration options. The panel is available on pages with a single pivot table layout as well as a layout with two data providers (chart only).

You can freely toggle between table and chart view and use the following features:

  • Select the chart type
  • Show or hide the legend and change its position
  • Enable or disable axis labels
  • Adjust the value axis baseline, including manually setting minimum and maximum values

For more information, see Chart Settings Side Panel and Chart Settings.

Figure 2: Chart Settings side panel

Enhanced Export Features

The Export function in review booklet and MDA apps now includes Google Workspace integration, Google Sheets format support, and a streamlined Export to PDF option. The following features are available:

Google Workspace Integration

You can use Google Workspace as a destination. When you activate cloud enablement, the system sets Cloud destination as the default option. Otherwise, Local is the only available destination. For more information, see Google Workspace Integration.

Google Sheets Format Support

When you integrate Google Workspace with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, you can export data in Google Sheets format. For more information, see Google Workspace Integration.

Figure 3: Google Sheets format support

Streamlined Export to PDF Function

The styling of PDF reports has been refined to improve readability and visual consistency.

Export to Spreadsheet

The exported spreadsheet file contains two sheets: The data sheet contains all tabular data with comments if you select them in the configuration dialog. And the Information on Query sheet lists all query-relevant information, for example, Tenant URL and Query ID.

Export to PDF

The exported PDF report supports both single data provider and two data providers. You can use the PDF configuration dialog to choose which sections to export.

For more information, see  Enhanced Export Features and Export Data to Spredsheet and PDF.

Migrate Variants and Bookmarks App

Using this app, you can migrate multidimensional data grid (Web Dynpro Grid) app variants and bookmarks to the corresponding Multidimensional Analysis (MDA) apps.

If you've been using Web Dynpro Grid reporting applications and your organization is moving to Multidimensional Analysis (MDA) applications, you can keep your saved variants and bookmarks by the Migrate Variants and Bookmarks app.

Your existing variants (saved filter settings) and bookmarks (saved report views with specific data selections) from Web Dynpro Grid reports can be transferred to MDA apps. You can continue working with your personalized report settings without recreating them manually.

 

 


Figure 4: The Migrate Variants and Bookmarks app

For more information, see Migrate Variants and Bookmarks (What's New) and Migrate Variants and Bookmarks (app description).

Usability Improvements

Typeahead in the Filter Bar

While you're entering a filter criterion in one of the filter fields in review booklet and MDA apps, the system proposes available entries to you. Click one of the proposals to select it.

This helps you find the right filter values faster, reduces typing effort, and avoids errors by ensuring you select valid entries. For more information about booklet filters, see Typeahead in Filter Bar and Working with Booklet Filters.

Enhanced App State Handling

Review booklet apps offer enhanced app state handling: Whenever you modify the layout, adjust variables, apply filters, or switch pages, review booklets now generate a new app state that is visible in the URL.

If the application times out, you can simply refresh the browser to restore your session. This ensures that ongoing work is not lost and provides a seamless recovery experience. As a result, review booklets now behave consistently with standard Fiori applications when it comes to session handling and state restoration. Multidimensional Analysis applications already supported automatic state creation during user interactions.

Previously, review booklets did not automatically generate a new app state when you made changes. An updated state was only created when one of the three sharing options (Send E-Mail, Microsoft Teams, Save as Tile) was used.

For more information, see Enhanced App State Handling and Working with Views.

 

What's New in the Review Booklet Designer

Flexible Content Types for Review Booklets with Multiple Data Providers

When configuring the business pages of a custom review booklet with two data providers, you can choose different content types for each data provider. By selecting Choose Content Type, a dialog opens where separate display options can be defined for the two data providers. For example, users can now configure:

  • A table for one data provider and a chart for the other data provider
  • Two different tables for the two data providers
  • Two different chart types for the two data providers

For more information, see Flexible Content Types for Review Booklets with Multiple Data Providers and Business Pages.

WYSIWYG Editor Support for Pages with Two Data Providers

When you configure a custom review booklet with two data providers, you can use the WYSIWYG mode.

Previously, the WYSIWYG editor allowed users to create pages that contained one data provider only. This restricted users in their ability to design more complex or comparative layouts directly within the editor.

With the latest enhancement, the WYSIWYG editor now fully supports pages that include two data providers. When you create or edit such a page, a dialog is available where you can individually configure the left and right side of the layout. This includes selecting different layouts or visualization options for each side, offering much greater flexibility in designing review booklet content.

For more information on your layout options, see WYSIWYG Editor Support for Pages with Two Data Providers and Save Layout in WYSIWYG Mode.

 

Figure 5: Pages with two data providers in the WSYIWIG editor

The latest enhancements to Review Booklets, MDA applications, and the Review Booklet Designer further strengthen SAP's self-service analytics capabilities in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. Whether you're analyzing key business metrics, creating executive-ready review booklets, or designing sophisticated multi-provider layouts, these new features offer greater flexibility, improved usability, and a more seamless user experience.

Explore the new capabilities in your system and visit the SAP Help Portal documentation to learn more about how these innovations can help you transform data into actionable insights:

Review Booklets

Multidimensional Analysis

Creating Review Booklets in the Review Booklet Designer

entry.title: Re: Onboarding 2.0 Limitation for Sweden to US Transfer/R...
entry.content:
The issue is rehire on old employment for future dated terminations is not supported.  The reason is a a technical one, when you do the rehire there isn;t technically a terminated record to do the rehire on,

SAP best practice you really should be using rehire on a new employment for country transfers.  As most payroll systems need a hire record for the employment and you will have have a rehire record in the USA not a hire record,

Rehire on a new employment works perfectly and generates the I9s!

entry.title: Re: New custom Application Job features and using Notification channel
entry.content:
Thank you, I appreciate the demonstration of the isolated notification feature.

As additional context, the interface itself has been released under the C1 contract since 2023 and has been available for Developer Extensibility scenarios across Public Cloud, Private Cloud, and On-Premise environments since then. Integration of this exit with the SAP Fiori Launchpad Notification Framework is one possible example of an action that can be executed when the exit is triggered.

It is also worth noting that a "notification" does not necessarily have to mean a message presented to a human user. Depending on the implementation, the notification exit could simply serve as an entry point that triggers an automated clean-up, recovery, or self-healing process implemented in code and executed when a particular technical Application Job state is reached.

As a lookout, with IBP we are already in a pilot phase. If everything proceeds as planned, the SAP Fiori Launchpad Notification Framework integration is expected to become a built-in capability with S/4HANA Public Cloud release 2702 and S/4HANA Private Cloud / On-Premise release 2027. In such a scenario, custom development for the specific purpose of posting notifications through the SAP Fiori Launchpad Notification Framework may no longer be required.

In addition to defining recurrence patterns (done in Step 2. Scheduling Options), users will be able to specify for which technical Job states (Completed, Completed with Error, Cancelled, Skipped) notifications should be generated directly by the framework itself, either via e-mail to one or multiple recipients or via the notification framework of Fiori Launchpad (which could be just the header icon or as well could be an e-mail notification). The difference is the Fiori Launchpad options notifies the logged in Business User "Notify Me" while in the native e-mail notification you can specify foreign recipients.

entry.title: Re: To use performance (MBO) rating as a direct payout %...
entry.content: Petre, I don't know the specifics of your particular configuration. However, the individual target on the summary (entry) level will be the aggregate of all the assignment level targets. For example, if you have a Assignment 1: 50% prorated assignment where the overall target is 10,000 and your plan is 50% Business and 50% Individual and Assignment 2 50% prorated assignment with a 10,000 target and 60% business and 40 % individual. The entry level individual target will calculate 2,500 from Assignment 1 (50% of 5,000 - prorated from the total target) and 2,000 from Assignment 2 (40% of 5,000 - prorated from the total target) for an individual target of 4,500. This is the math. If you are seeing anything differently, you need to open a ticket. In general, each assignment will prorate a total assignment target and assign section targets based on the percentages prescribed in the plan. The entry will be a summation of all of the assignments.

entry.title: Re: Problems with reporting period of Vietnam Balance She...
entry.content:
Hi @Hide94 ,

Short answer: This is most likely a configuration issue in the report-category periodicity, not a hard product limitation — the wrong From/To Period comes from the fiscal year variant that drives the reporting-period derivation, and that can be corrected before you conclude it's a limitation of the standard report.

How the period parameters are derived (root cause)

The reporting period you pick on the task is not read directly from your company code fiscal year. It is derived from the Fiscal Year Variant assigned to the report category in Set Periodicity of Report Category (part of SSCUI 101071). SAP explicitly states this variant "is only used to define the reporting periods, and is independent of the company code fiscal year" (Setting Up Your Statutory Reporting, SAP Help).

So if the report category still carries the SAP default (a calendar-based variant, K4), the app maps your task to calendar periods — which is exactly why 2026/01–2026/12 returns data and your real fiscal year 2025/08–2026/07 comes back blank with From/To Period that don't match your 001–012. (This part is a deduction from your symptoms, as I do not have access to your system, and in my Starter, Vietnam is not activated, so I cannot verify by myself. Please verify it in your system as below.)

Is this a limitation of the default statutory report?

Not confirmed — check configuration first. The standard SAP-delivered report entity/report definition content is read-only in Public Cloud, but the period derivation is driven by configuration you can change, so it should not be assumed to be a limitation until the FY variant and parameter mapping are ruled out.

Is there a way to solve it?

Yes, with limits. Work through these steps:

Verify: Open Analyze Data, note the derived Fiscal Year + From/To Period, and compare against where your data actually sits (fiscal periods 001–012 of FY 2025 under your Aug–Jul variant). This confirms whether the mismatch is calendar-vs-fiscal.

Fix in config: In Set Periodicity of Report Category, set the report category FY Variant to one whose periods align with your Aug–Jul fiscal year (period 001 = August), instead of the default calendar variant (Setting Up Your Statutory Reporting).

Constraint: You cannot change periodicity while tasks already exist — first delete the generated tasks with the Statutory Reporting Task Cleaner (app Clean Up Reporting Tasks), then adjust and regenerate.

Parameter override: If the report exposes From/To Period as parameters, review Enter Parameters Specific to the Report Category (SSCUI 101071); parameters can also be maintained via the Define Statutory Reports app on a copied report definition (Enter Parameters Specific to the Report Category).

If still blank: A no-data result on a technically valid run is covered by SAP KBA 3700823. If the standard reporting activity genuinely derives the period from the calendar and can't be corrected in config, raise an SAP incident under component FI-LOC-SRF-RUN to confirm non-calendar FY support for this Vietnam report.

Summary

Don't assume a product limitation yet. Check the derived period in Analyze Data, then align the report-category Fiscal Year Variant to your Aug–Jul fiscal year in Set Periodicity of Report Category (delete existing tasks first via the Task Cleaner). If the period is still forced to calendar after that, open an SAP incident (FI-LOC-SRF-RUN) to confirm whether the standard VN_FIN_STMNT content supports a non-calendar fiscal year.

Hope it helps.
Best regards,
Michal

entry.title: Re: How to correct a RAR posting, when position in sales...
entry.content: In SAP RAR, once a sales order item has been deleted without a reason code and revenue recognition has already been processed, you cannot simply “remove” the item by changing RAR relevance. The corrective approach is to cancel or adjust the performance obligation in RAR using contract modification or reversal logic, rather than trying to alter the material master relevance. Use RAR’s contract modification/cancellation functionality to reverse the performance obligation and revenue postings. Avoid manual deletion or relevance changes, as these are not supported in productive systems.

entry.title: Re: I can't receive (with MIGO) collective orders with se...
entry.content:
Without serial numbers, the system can post the goods receipt collectively, update the order quantity, and set status to DLV. But with serial numbers, each unit must be individually received with a serial number assignment. The collective order logic does not bypass serial number checks. That is why you get the error “No GR possible: Order is part of collective order” and the job stays stuck.
In short, you need to receive the jobs via confirmation or MIGO with serial number assignment, not through the collective order tree alone.

entry.title: No More Workarounds at the Receiving Dock
entry.content:
A Quick Recap: ASR Unloading on the RF Device

In the Advanced Shipping and Receiving scenario, the inbound process follows a well-defined sequence. Once a freight order is created and the truck is checked in at the receiving point, the warehouse worker takes over at the RF device to execute the physical unloading — scanning each handling unit (HU) as it comes off the truck and confirming it against the expected freight order content.

This handshake between TM and EWM is what makes ASR powerful: the freight order drives the warehouse execution, status updates flow both ways, and everything stays in sync. The warehouse monitor, the Fiori app "Load or Unload Freight Orders," and the RF UI all reflect the same picture.

Before FPS1 2025, the ASR RF unloading transaction offered no structured exception path when an HU was missing or when stock arrived in an unexpected packing format. Warehouse workers had to exit the RF flow, escalate to the office, and fix the discrepancy manually — in a transaction that was never designed for that. For high-throughput warehouses running on lean staffing, that is a costly interruption.

What FPS1 2025 Delivers: HUMI and CRHU

SAP S/4HANA 2025 FPS1 introduces two new capabilities in the ASR RF unloading flow, delivered under roadmap item INNO_64055 (application component TM-FRM-ASR-LUF):

Exception Code HUMI — "This HU is not here"

HUMI is a new exception code available in the ASR RF UI during unloading. It is triggered when a handling unit that is registered in the freight order — and therefore expected to arrive — is physically absent from the truck.

With HUMI, the worker can now flag the exception directly on the RF device. The system registers that the specific HU was expected but not received, and the freight order and inbound delivery reflect that status. The process can continue for the HUs that are present — no full stop, no manual cleanup session after the fact.

Practical scenario: A truck from a regular supplier arrives with 11 of 12 expected pallets. The 12th pallet was left behind at the origin warehouse. The RF worker scans the 11 pallets, reaches the missing one, triggers HUMI, and the unloading process closes cleanly for what was received. The freight order records the discrepancy. The procurement and logistics teams see the shortfall immediately — without anyone needing to call the supervisor over.

 Function Button CRHU — "This stock is packed, but the system does not know it"

CRHU (Create HU) is a new function button in the ASR RF unloading flow that addresses a mirror-image problem. Here, the system records items as loose, unpacked stock — but physically, the delivery arrives on pallets or in cartons with an HU that does not yet exist in EWM.

When stock originates from a supplier that does not transmit HU-level packing data, EWM holds the delivery items at the item level with no associated handling units. The physical delivery, however, might be fully palletized. Without CRHU, the only way to reconcile this was a post-unloading HU creation in a desktop transaction — a step that pulled people off the floor.

With CRHU available directly on the RF device, the worker can create the handling unit on the spot, associate it with the correct delivery items, and keep the physical flow and the system in sync — all without leaving the RF transaction.

Practical scenario: A supplier ships 200 units. The ASN contains item-level data but no packing information. EWM expects 200 individual pieces. The truck arrives with 10 pallets of 20 units each. The RF worker triggers CRHU for each pallet, creates the HUs directly on screen, and the stock is received with the correct packing structure from the start — no rework in the putaway zone needed.

 Why This Matters for ASR Deployments Specifically

In non-ASR inbound flows, EWM has had exception handling mechanisms for some time. The gap was specifically in the ASR context — where the freight order from TM drives the RF unloading flow — and this is where FPS1 2025 closes it.

For customers running the Advanced TM-EWM Inbound ASR process (scope item 7VE), these additions directly improve one of the most operationally sensitive steps in the end-to-end flow. The key benefits confirmed by SAP for INNO_64055 are:

  • Improved process flow for warehouse workers during unloading — the RF flow no longer has a dead end when reality diverges from the plan.
  • Reduced clerical work — discrepancies are handled at the point of occurrence, not discovered and corrected in a separate back-office session.

 Availability and Configuration

 

Parameter

 

Detail

 

Available since

 

SAP S/4HANA 2025 FPS1 (Q1 2026)

 

Deployment models

 

SAP S/4HANA on-premise  |  SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Private Edition

 

Application component

 

TM-FRM-ASR-LUF (Loading and Unloading Freight Orders)

 

Activation required

 

No separate licensing activation — available with ASR RF unloading

 

Roadmap innovation ID

 

INNO_64055

 

SAP Help Portal

 

https://help.sap.com/whats-new/5fc51e30e2744f168642e26e0c1d9be1

 What else is new for ASR in FPS1 2025?

HUMI and CRHU are not the only ASR enhancements in FPS1 2025. The same release also delivers:

BAdIs for prechecks before loading execution (INNO_64044)

Customer-specific validation logic invoked before loading starts — in the RF screen, the Fiori "Load or Unload Freight Orders" app, and the EWM desktop transaction /SCWM/LOAD. A clean, supported extensibility point for business-specific loading conditions.

Other process and UX updates (INNO_64046)

Enhanced freight-order search in wave maintenance (/SCWM/WAVE) and the warehouse monitor (/SCWM/MON); weight and volume correction for unpacked items in outbound delivery orders; synchronization of actual EWM weight/volume back into TM documents.

Decentral integration with SAP GTS (INNO_60203)

Full SAP Global Trade Services integration for the side-by-side TM+EWM ASR scenario: compliance checks, customs declarations, unloading permission requests, and transit closure for both inbound and outbound flows.

The Bottom Line

If your team has been working around ASR unloading limitations — coaching RF workers through manual exception procedures, running post-unload cleanup sessions, or accepting stock accuracy gaps because the RF flow had no graceful exit — FPS1 2025 is worth a close look.

HUMI and CRHU are not glamorous features. They will not make the headline in any conference keynote. But in the warehouses where ASR is live, they will quietly eliminate two of the most common sources of friction in the inbound receiving workflow. 
That is exactly the kind of improvement that makes a real difference on the floor.

 References:

SAP Help Portal — What's New, Supply Chain > Warehousing:
https://help.sap.com/whats-new/5fc51e30e2744f168642e26e0c1d9be1?Line_of_Business=Supply+Chain&Business_Area=Warehousing

SAP Roadmap Explorer — INNO_64055 (Q1 2026):
https://roadmaps.sap.com/board?range=CURRENT-LAST#Q1%202026;INNO=87D39AF950211EDFAFA3AE22EF085974

entry.content:
Hi Community,

Thanks and best regards,
Katherine

entry.title: Re: Batch characteristic to be transferred from outbound...
entry.content:
This is a well‑known limitation in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud. Batch characteristics maintained in the outbound delivery are not automatically transferred to the newly created batch in the inbound delivery. The system treats the outbound batch and the inbound batch as separate entities, so the “Heat number” characteristic does not flow by default.

The Best Practice recommendation is use Batch Derivation to automatically transfer the “Heat number” from the raw material batch in the outbound delivery to the FG batch created in the inbound delivery. This avoids manual entry and ensures compliance with traceability requirements.

entry.title: Mastering Dynamic Multi-Step Approvals in SAP S/4HANA Flexible Workflow: A Practical Guide
entry.content:
Solution Architecture

The design separates workflow behaviour into two independent layers.

1. Workflow Step Evaluation Layer

Implemented through: SWF_WORKFLOW_CONDITION_EVAL 

This layer determines whether a workflow step should execute or be skipped.

It acts as the gatekeeper and controls the workflow path without creating unnecessary work items.

2. Agent Determination Layer

Implemented through: MMPUR_WORKFLOW_AGENTS_V2

This layer determines who receives the workflow task once the step is permitted to execute.

Shared Runtime State

Both layers rely on a custom field stored on the procurement document header.

This field acts as an approval state pointer that tracks the current stage of the approval process.

Phase 1: Create the Approval State Tracker

The first step is to create a custom field that stores the approval level currently reached by the document.

Create a Custom Field

Open the Custom Fields application and create a field with the following settings:

Setting Value
Business ContextProcurement: Core Scheduling Agreement Header
LabelApproval Level
TypeText (Length 2) or Integer

Example technical field: 

YY1_APPROVALLEVEL_PDH

After creating the field:

  1. Enable it for the relevant application.
  2. Save the configuration.
  3. Publish the field.

The field will now be available in CDS views and extensibility scenarios.

Phase 2: Create a Custom Workflow Condition

The next step is to make a custom condition available inside the Flexible Workflow configuration UI.

Create Condition Definition Logic

In the Custom Logic application, create a new implementation using: SWF_WORKFLOW_CONDITION_DEF


IF scenario_id = 'WS00800305'. APPEND VALUE #( id = 'Z_SKIP_APPROVER' subject = 'Skip Approver' type = if_swf_flex_ifs_condition_def=>cs_condtype-start_step ) TO ct_condition. APPEND VALUE #( id = 'Z_SKIP_APPROVER' name = 'AUTO_USER' shorttext = 'Based on dynamic level check' xsd_type = if_swf_flex_ifs_condition_def=>cs_xstype-string mandatory = abap_false ) TO ct_parameter. ENDIF.​

After publishing the implementation, the custom condition becomes available in the workflow step configuration screen.

 

Phase 3: Implement Workflow Step Evaluation

Now the custom condition needs runtime logic.

Create another implementation using: SWF_WORKFLOW_CONDITION_EVAL

This implementation evaluates whether the workflow step should run.
IF condition_id = 'Z_SKIP_APPROVER'. DATA(lv_doc_number) = id_object_key. SELECT SINGLE yy1_approvallevel_pdh FROM i_purdschedgagreeview WHERE professionalschedulingagreement = @LV_doc_number INTO @DATA(lv_current_level). IF lv_current_level <> '1'. cv_is_true = abap_false. ELSE. cv_is_true = abap_true. ENDIF. ENDIF.

What Happens Here?

If the document has not yet reached approval level 1: cv_is_true = abap_false

The workflow silently skips the step.

If the document has reached approval level 1:cv_is_true = abap_true

The workflow executes the step and generates a work item.

This approach allows step execution logic to remain completely dynamic.

Phase 4: Configure Dynamic Agent Determination

Once a step is approved for execution, the workflow engine calls the agent determination BAdI.

Implement: MMPUR_WORKFLOW_AGENTS_V2

within method:
IF_MMPUR_WORKFLOW_AGENTS_V2~GET_APPROVERS
 
METHOD if_mmpur_workflow_agents_v2~get_approvers. DATA: ls_approver TYPE if_mmpur_workflow_agents_v2=>bd_mmpur_s_badi_approver. CLEAR approverlist. SELECT SINGLE yy1_approvallevel_pdh FROM i_purdschedgagreeview WHERE professionalschedulingagreement = @purchasingdocument INTO @DATA(lv_level). CASE lv_level. WHEN '0' OR ''. ls_approver-businessuser = 'MANAGER_L1_ID'. ls_approver-approvallevel = 1. APPEND ls_approver TO approverlist. WHEN '1'. ls_approver-businessuser = 'MANAGER_L2_ID'. ls_approver-approvallevel = 2. APPEND ls_approver TO approverlist. ENDCASE. ENDMETHOD.
In this pattern:
  • Level 1 tasks are routed to the first approver.
  • Level 2 tasks are routed to the second approver.
  • Workflow routing remains entirely dynamic.

    Phase 5: Build the Workflow Sequence in Fiori

    With the extensibility components deployed, the workflow itself can now be configured in the standard application.

    Open: Manage Workflows for Scheduling Agreements 

    and create a new workflow template.

    Step 10 – Level 1 Review

    Configure:

    Recipients : BAdI Agent Determination ->

    Conditions :None

    This step always executes.

    Step 20 – Level 2 Approval

    Configure:

    Recipients BAdI Agent Determination

    Conditions - Skip Approver – Based on Dynamic Level Check

    This step executes only when the custom evaluation logic allows it.

    Activate the workflow.

    Phase 6: Advance the Approval State

    The final piece of the design is updating the approval level field after each approval action.

    Typical examples include:

    0 → 1 after Level 1 approval
    1 → 2 after Level 2 approval

    This update can be implemented using:

    • Workflow enhancement points
    • Process execution BAdIs
    • RAP actions
    • EML update operations
    • Procurement-specific enhancement implementations

      The exact implementation will depend on the document type and extensibility model used in your system.

      End-to-End Process Flow

      The resulting workflow behaves as follows:

      Initial Document Creation

      Approval Level = 0

      Workflow triggers.

      Step 10 executes.

      Task is routed to: MANAGER_L1_ID

      Level 1 Approval Completed

      Approval level updated: Approval Level = 1

      Workflow moves to Step 20.

      Step 20 Evaluation

      Custom condition evaluates:

      YY1_APPROVALLEVEL_PDH = '1'

      Result: cv_is_true = abap_true

      Step executes.

      Agent Determination

      The BAdI returns: MANAGER_L2_ID

      A new work item is created and routed to the Level 2 approver.

      If the approval level had not matched the expected value, the step would have been skipped automatically.

      Benefits of This Approach

      This pattern provides several advantages over traditional workflow development:

      • No classical workflow modelling required.
      • Fully compatible with SAP S/4HANA Flexible Workflow.
      • Business users maintain workflow layouts in Fiori.
      • Dynamic approval sequences can be configured without modifying workflow definitions.
      • Clear separation between routing logic and step execution logic.
      • Scalable for multiple approval levels and complex approval matrices.
      • Suitable for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, Private Edition, and On-Premise deployments.

        Conclusion

        Many organisations require approval flows where subsequent workflow steps depend on runtime decisions made during earlier approvals. Standard Flexible Workflow conditions alone cannot always address these requirements.

        By combining a custom state-tracking field, workflow precondition evaluation, and standard agent determination BAdIs, it is possible to build highly dynamic multi-level approval chains while remaining fully aligned with SAP’s extensibility framework.

        The result is a flexible, maintainable, and cloud-compliant workflow design that keeps business process configuration in the hands of workflow administrators while allowing developers to implement sophisticated approval routing with minimal custom code.

entry.title: SAP RAP : Dynamically manipulating fields based on Parent field value.
entry.content:
Step 1: Define Control Fields in CDS View

Create boolean control fields like isAppleBrand, isMaleGender in your CDS view.

define view ZMobileDevice as select from MobileDevice {
  key ID,
  Brand,
  ModelVariant,
  Gender,
  case Brand when 'Apple' then abap_true else abap_false end as isAppleBrand,
  case Gender when 'Male' then abap_true else abap_false end as isMaleGender
}

 

Step 2: Use Annotations for Conditional Visibility

Use @ui.Hidden annotation with control fields.

@ui.hidden: 'isAppleBrand'
ModelVariant

@UI.hidden: 'isMaleGender'
PregnancyStatus

@UI.hidden: 'isMaleGender'
MaternityLeave

Step 3: Enable Side Effects

Ensure side effects are triggered when the parent field changes. Handle this in Behaviour Definition.

determination setVisibility on modify { field Brand, Model;  }
  side effects { field Brand affects field isAppleBrand; }

 

Step 4: Behaviour Implementation 

METHOD setVisiblity.
    DATA: lt_update TYPE TABLE FOR UPDATE <root view>.

    READ ENTITIES OF <root view> IN LOCAL MODE
        ENTITY <entity>
        FIELDS ( Brand Model )
        WITH CORRESPONDING #( keys )
        RESULT DATA(lt_mob_data).


    DATA lt_update_fields TYPE TABLE FOR UPDATE <root view>.
    DATA ls_update_fields LIKE LINE OF lt_update_fields.

*
data(ls_entity) = lt_mob_data[ 1 ]-entity.


    lt_update = VALUE #( FOR ls_mob IN lt_mob_data (
          %tky = ls_mob-%tky

          " Set the boolean field based on ProjectType
          IsAppleBrand = xsdbool( ls_pir-Brande <> '01' OR ls_pir-Brand IS INITIAL )
         
          " Use the control structure to signal which fields should be updated
          %control = VALUE #(
                              IsAppleBrand = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
                               )
    ) ) ).

    MODIFY ENTITIES OF <rootview> IN LOCAL MODE
         ENTITY <entity>
         UPDATE FROM lt_update
         REPORTED DATA(lt_reported).

    reported = CORRESPONDING #( DEEP lt_reported ).


  ENDMETHOD.

Dynamic field control in SAP RAP enhances user experience by tailoring the UI to the context. Whether you're building a mobile configuration app or a gender-sensitive form, these techniques help you deliver clean, relevant interfaces.

entry.title: Re: Inspection lot sample size calculation based on manua...
entry.content: In S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, sampling cannot be driven by batch characteristics or custom fields. It is a standard limitation. The recommended workaround is to use sample-drawing procedures or manual adjustment, and if automation is essential, build a side-by-side extension to propose carton-based sample sizes.

entry.title: GR Label Printing Based on “Number of Labels” Field
entry.content:
Hi Team,

Currently, the GR label printing process generates labels based on the received quantity. For example, if a line item has a quantity of 100, the system automatically prints 100 labels.

The client would like to change this behavior so that the number of labels printed is determined by the “Number of Labels” field entered by the user, rather than the received quantity.

Example:

  • Quantity = 100
  • Number of Labels = 2

Expected Result:

  • The system should print 2 labels only, based on the value entered in the “Number of Labels” field.

In summary, the label printing logic should be controlled by the “Number of Labels” field, enabling users to specify exactly how many labels need to be printed for each GR line item.

Please advise on the feasibility of implementing this requirement and the recommended approach.

Thanks & Regards,
Kiran
